name: Assistant
mes:
## The operative distinction

ŌĆ£Working with PDFsŌĆØ splits into a few different jobs: generating new PDFs, editing existing PDFs, extracting text/tables/layout, and rendering pages for viewing. The best tool depends much more on that job than on the language alone. Based on the current mainstream open-source landscape, Python is stronger for extraction/analysis/repair workflows, while JavaScript is especially strong for browser rendering and client-side PDF generation/editing. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn744067search12Ņłéturn744067search1Ņłéturn744067search2Ņłéturn744067search11Ņłéturn566257search1Ņłéturn566257search12Ņłéturn566257search2Ņłéturn642725search2Ņłü

## Python

For **general-purpose PDF editing** in Python, the default mainstream choice is **pypdf**. Its docs describe it as a pure-Python library for splitting, merging, cropping, transforming pages, adding metadata/viewing options/passwords, and retrieving text/metadata. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn744067search12Ņłéturn744067search0Ņłü

For **lower-level PDF object work, repair, and more spec-near manipulation**, **pikepdf** is the sharper tool. It is built on **qpdf**, which its project describes as a mature, actively maintained C++ PDF library. That makes it a good fit when pypdf feels too high-level or too forgiving. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn744067search1Ņłü

For **fast extraction, page rendering, conversion, and manipulation**, **PyMuPDF** is one of the strongest Python options. Its docs describe it as a high-performance library for extraction, analysis, conversion, and manipulation; its GitHub page also notes the open-source **AGPL** and commercial licensing model, which matters if you are shipping closed-source software. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn744067search11Ņłéturn744067search3Ņłéturn744067search7Ņłü

For **text/layout/table extraction**, the usual Python names are **pdfplumber** and **pdfminer.six**. pdfplumber is built on pdfminer.six and is aimed at detailed access to characters, rectangles, and lines, plus table extraction and visual debugging; pdfminer.six is the lower-level text-analysis/extraction tool with access to positions, fonts, and colors. In practice, pdfplumber is usually the nicer first stop when you need structured extraction from machine-generated PDFs. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn744067search2Ņłéturn744067search14Ņłéturn642725search1Ņłéturn642725search9Ņłü

For **creating PDFs from scratch** in Python, **ReportLab** is still one of the standard names. Its docs describe it as a library for directly creating PDFs and graphics from Python. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn642725search12Ņłéturn642725search0Ņłü

## JavaScript

For **rendering/viewing PDFs in the browser**, the standard answer is **PDF.js**. Mozilla describes it as a general-purpose, web-standards-based platform for parsing and rendering PDFs, and the npm package **pdfjs-dist** is the prebuilt distribution most JS projects actually install. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn566257search1Ņłéturn566257search5Ņłéturn642725search3Ņłéturn642725search19Ņłü

For **creating and modifying PDFs in JavaScript across runtimes**, **pdf-lib** is one of the cleanest mainstream choices. Its docs state that it works in Node, browsers, Deno, and React Native, and supports creating PDFs from scratch or modifying existing ones. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn566257search0Ņłéturn566257search12Ņłéturn642725search22Ņłü

For **programmatic PDF generation** in JS, **PDFKit** is a long-standing standard, especially on the Node side. Its project describes itself as a generation library for Node and the browser, with both low-level functions and higher-level abstractions; its docs also cover form creation. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn566257search2Ņłéturn566257search6Ņłéturn566257search18Ņłü

For **browser-first PDF generation**, **jsPDF** is still widely used. It is explicitly aimed at generating PDFs in JavaScript on the client side. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn566257search3Ņłéturn566257search23Ņłü

For **declarative PDF generation** from structured content, **pdfmake** is a strong option. Its docs describe server-side and client-side generation in pure JavaScript with a document-definition style API. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn642725search2Ņłéturn642725search6Ņłéturn642725search10Ņłü

## Best current defaults

If you want the shortest honest answer:

- **Python**
  - **pypdf** for merge/split/basic edits
  - **pikepdf** for lower-level repair/object manipulation
  - **PyMuPDF** for speed, rendering, and extraction-heavy workflows
  - **pdfplumber** for table/text/layout extraction
  - **ReportLab** for generating PDFs from scratch ŅłĆciteŅłéturn744067search12Ņłéturn744067search1Ņłéturn744067search11Ņłéturn744067search2Ņłéturn642725search12Ņłü

- **JavaScript**
  - **PDF.js / pdfjs-dist** for viewing/rendering/parsing
  - **pdf-lib** for editing/filling/creating PDFs in app code
  - **PDFKit** or **pdfmake** for generated documents
  - **jsPDF** for browser-side export/download flows ŅłĆciteŅłéturn566257search1Ņłéturn642725search3Ņłéturn566257search12Ņłéturn566257search2Ņłéturn642725search2Ņłéturn566257search3Ņłü

## What professionals usually do

A very common real-world pattern is:

- **Frontend viewer**: PDF.js
- **Frontend edit/fill/stamp/merge**: pdf-lib
- **Backend extraction pipeline**: Python, often PyMuPDF and/or pdfplumber/pdfminer.six
- **Backend generated reports/invoices**: ReportLab in Python, or PDFKit/pdfmake in JS depending on the stack

That last point is an inference from the capabilities and positioning of these tools rather than a single source stating ŌĆ£the industry standard stack is X,ŌĆØ but it matches the current ecosystem pretty well. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn566257search1Ņłéturn566257search12Ņłéturn744067search11Ņłéturn744067search2Ņłéturn642725search1Ņłéturn642725search12Ņłéturn566257search2Ņłéturn642725search2Ņłü

## My blunt recommendation

If your goal is **serious PDF extraction or document forensics**, start with **Python**. If your goal is **browser-based viewing/editing/export**, start with **JavaScript**. If your goal is **full-stack PDF work**, the most practical combo is usually **PDF.js + pdf-lib on the JS side** and **PyMuPDF/pdfplumber/pypdf on the Python side**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn566257search1Ņłéturn566257search12Ņłéturn744067search11Ņłéturn744067search2Ņłéturn744067search12Ņłü

If you want, I can turn this into a tighter **task-by-task cheat sheet** like ŌĆ£extract text,ŌĆØ ŌĆ£fill forms,ŌĆØ ŌĆ£convert pages to images,ŌĆØ ŌĆ£merge/split,ŌĆØ ŌĆ£parse tables,ŌĆØ and ŌĆ£generate reports,ŌĆØ with the exact best library for each in Python and JS.

name: Assistant
mes:
## For **accessible PDF work specifically**

The blunt answer is: **Python and JavaScript can help a lot, but they do not replace the normal accessibility workflow for real PDFs**. The current guidance in the docs you uploaded is consistent on the big points: start with an accessible source document, preserve tags on export, prefer HTML or web forms when you can, and expect Acrobat Pro plus manual review for serious remediation work. Searchable text, tags, reading order, alt text, title/language, and accessible form behavior are all part of the job. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn1file17Ņłü ŅłĆciteŅłéturn653071search5Ņłéturn653071search11Ņłü

## What Python is good at

Python is strong for the **pipeline parts**:

- checking whether text is selectable
- OCRing scanned PDFs
- extracting text, images, metadata, outlines, and form fields
- batch auditing for obvious failures
- making repetitive low-risk fixes
- generating PDFs from structured data

That fits the uploaded guidance pretty well, because the first triage questions are often ŌĆ£is this just an image?ŌĆØ and ŌĆ£does it have tags?ŌĆØ, and OCR plus automated checks are useful early steps even though they are not sufficient for full remediation. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file0Ņłü

For libraries, the current mainstream Python tools are still things like **pypdf**, **pikepdf**, and **PyMuPDF** for general PDF manipulation, inspection, repair, and extraction. PyMuPDF also exposes some lower-level tagged-PDF-related data such as `/MarkInfo`, which is useful if you are inspecting structure-related flags programmatically. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn635363search7Ņłéturn589136search4Ņłü

Where Python gets weaker is **true accessible-PDF authoring/remediation in the open-source mainstream**. The clearest officially documented Python options I found are commercial or partly commercial:

- **Aspose.PDF for Python via .NET** explicitly documents creating tagged PDFs, validating against **PDF/UA-1**, converting to PDF/UA with automatic tagging, and creating accessible tagged form fields. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn668866view0Ņłü
- **ReportLab** has accessibility documentation, but its own docs say tagging is available in **ReportLab Plus** rather than the open-source edition. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn985471view1Ņłü

So for Python, my honest read is:

- **excellent** for OCR, extraction, preflight, batch inspection, and glue code
- **possible** for accessible generation if you use a commercial stack like Aspose
- **not a great replacement** for Acrobat-level remediation of arbitrary existing PDFs ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file0Ņłü ŅłĆciteŅłéturn668866view0Ņłéturn985471view1Ņłü

## What JavaScript is good at

JavaScript is strongest when the PDF work is tied to the **browser or app UI**.

- **PDF.js** is the standard viewer/parsing stack. Its own docs frame it as a core/display/viewer system for parsing and rendering PDFs, which makes it great for viewing, inspection tooling, and custom in-browser experiences. It is not primarily an accessibility-remediation engine. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn985471view5Ņłéturn589136search7Ņłü
- **PDFKit** is the most promising JavaScript option I found for **generating accessible PDFs from scratch**. Its docs explicitly have an accessibility section and document options for marked content, logical structure, Tagged PDF, and **PDF/UA** such as `tagged: true` and `subset: 'PDF/UA'`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn985471view0Ņłéturn970249search4Ņłü

The big caution on the JS side is **editing existing tagged PDFs while preserving accessibility structure**. I would be careful with **pdf-lib** for that use case. Its GitHub issue tracker and discussions include reports that tagged accessibility structure can be lost when pages are copied or when a manipulated PDF is saved. That is not the same as an official ŌĆ£we do not support thisŌĆØ statement, but it is enough that I would not choose it for accessibility-preserving remediation. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn985471view2Ņłéturn985471view3Ņłü

So for JavaScript, my read is:

- **excellent** for viewing and app-side workflows
- **pretty good** for generating new accessible PDFs if you control the document structure and use PDFKit carefully
- **risky** for preserving accessibility tags during arbitrary PDF edits with general-purpose libraries like pdf-lib ŅłĆciteŅłéturn985471view0Ņłéturn985471view2Ņłéturn985471view3Ņłü

## The practical boundary

The uploaded accessibility guidance matters more than the language choice:

- If the PDF is **scanned**, code can help with OCR, but OCR output usually still needs more work. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file0Ņłü
- If the PDF lacks **tags** or has bad **reading order**, full remediation becomes much more manual and Acrobat-heavy. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file2Ņłü
- If it is a **form**, accessible naming, labeling, and tab order are specialized work; the guidance you uploaded is pretty explicit that PDF forms usually require substantial Acrobat work. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file0Ņłü
- Accessibility checkers help, but they do **not** reliably judge reading order quality or whether alt text is actually useful. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file0Ņłü ŅłĆciteŅłéturn653071search5Ņłü

## My actual recommendation

If your goal is **batch analysis, OCR, preflight, and remediation support tooling**, I would lean **Python**.

If your goal is **creating new PDFs inside a web app**, I would lean **JavaScript with PDFKit**.

If your goal is **remediating messy real-world PDFs to accessible production quality**, I would use **code for triage and automation**, but still plan on **Acrobat Pro/manual QA** for the final accessibility work. That is the direction your uploaded sources point to, and I think theyŌĆÖre right. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn1file14Ņłü

If you want, I can turn this into a **task-by-task matrix** like:

- detect inaccessible PDFs
- OCR scanned PDFs
- generate accessible PDFs from structured data
- preserve tags during edits
- accessible form workflows
- best Python stack
- best JavaScript stack
swipes:

If your goal is **remediating messy real-world PDFs to accessible production quality**, I would use **code for triage and automation**, but still plan on **Acrobat Pro/manual QA** for the final accessibility work. That is the direction your uploaded sources point to, and I think theyŌĆÖre right. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn2file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn1file14Ņłü

If you want, I can turn this into a **task-by-task matrix** like:

- detect inaccessible PDFs
- OCR scanned PDFs
- generate accessible PDFs from structured data
- preserve tags during edits
- accessible form workflows
- best Python stack
- best JavaScript stack

name: You
mes: You know how Office documents use OOXML, with their own ML per type of file? What does PDF use?
swipes: You know how Office documents use OOXML, with their own ML per type of file? What does PDF use?

name: Assistant
mes: IŌĆÖm checking the exact standards language here, because the answer is a little counterintuitive: PDF does have its own internal syntax and object model, but it is not ŌĆ£the PDF version of XMLŌĆØ in the way WordprocessingML or SpreadsheetML are.
swipes: IŌĆÖm checking the exact standards language here, because the answer is a little counterintuitive: PDF does have its own internal syntax and object model, but it is not ŌĆ£the PDF version of XMLŌĆØ in the way WordprocessingML or SpreadsheetML are.

name: Assistant
mes:
## The distinction

Office files use **OOXML**, which is a **ZIP package of XML parts** like WordprocessingML, SpreadsheetML, and PresentationML.

**PDF does not work that way.** It does **not** have a parallel ŌĆ£PDFMLŌĆØ that serves as the main document format. The core format is just **PDF itself**, standardized as **ISO 32000** (current core spec: **PDF 2.0 / ISO 32000-2**). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn927809search0Ņłéturn927809search1Ņłü

## What PDF uses instead

PDF is best thought of as an **object-based page description format** with its own syntax, not an XML vocabulary. PDF Association describes it as an **object-based page description language**. A PDF file is made of PDF objects such as dictionaries, arrays, strings, numbers, streams, and indirect objects, plus cross-reference data that lets readers find those objects quickly. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn927809search4Ņłéturn300958search1Ņłü

The part that actually draws a page is the **content stream**. AdobeŌĆÖs PDF Reference describes a content stream as a stream object containing a **sequence of instructions** that describe the graphical elements to paint on a page, interpreted sequentially according to PDF syntax rules. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn927809search34Ņłü

So the closest honest mapping is:

- **DOCX/XLSX/PPTX** ŌåÆ ZIP container + XML parts
- **PDF** ŌåÆ native PDF object syntax + streams + operators + xref structure ŅłĆciteŅłéturn300958search1Ņłéturn927809search34Ņłü

## The closest things to ŌĆ£markupŌĆØ inside PDF

There are a few things that look *somewhat* like separate sublanguages, but none of them are the main equivalent of OOXML:

- **Content stream operators**  
  These are the drawing/text operators inside page content streams. This is probably the closest thing to ŌĆ£PDFŌĆÖs native page markup,ŌĆØ but it is not XML. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn927809search34Ņłü

- **Tagged PDF structure**  
  For accessibility and logical structure, PDF can include a **structure tree** with tags and relationships. Adobe describes a logical tagged structure tree as the mechanism that provides meaningful reading order and defines structural elements and relationships. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü

- **XMP metadata**  
  PDF often embeds **XMP**, which *is* XML-based metadata. But that is metadata inside the PDF, not the PDF document format itself. PDF Association notes that PDF supports an extensible metadata model, and embedded metadata in PDF is commonly XMP. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn927809search0Ņłéturn927809search4Ņłü

- **FDF / XFDF for forms data**  
  These are sidecar/interchange formats for PDF form data. **XFDF** is XML-based, but again, it is not the core PDF file format. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn927809search0Ņłü

## So the direct answer

The PDF equivalent is **not an ML dialect like WordprocessingML**.

The real answer is:

**PDF uses the PDF file format itself** ŌĆö an **ISO-standardized object-and-stream syntax** with **content streams** for page instructions, optional **tagged structure** for semantics/accessibility, and optional **XMP XML** for metadata. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn927809search0Ņłéturn927809search1Ņłéturn927809search34Ņłü

## If youŌĆÖre thinking like a developer

A useful mental model is:

- **OOXML** = ŌĆ£document as XML packageŌĆØ
- **PDF** = ŌĆ£document as serialized object graph plus drawing instruction streamsŌĆØ

That difference is why low-level PDF work feels closer to parsing a graphics/page-description format than walking an XML DOM. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn300958search1Ņłéturn927809search34Ņłü

If you want, I can do the next layer and show you the rough internal anatomy of a PDF file ŌĆö header, indirect objects, streams, xref table/stream, trailer, page tree, and content stream operators ŌĆö side by side with the OOXML mental model.

name: You
mes: Where do I find the guide for the object based page description format, language?
swipes: Where do I find the guide for the object based page description format, language?

name: Assistant
mes: IŌĆÖm pulling the primary specs and the most usable guides. ThereŌĆÖs a difference between the official standard and the human-readable introductions, and you probably want both.
swipes: IŌĆÖm pulling the primary specs and the most usable guides. ThereŌĆÖs a difference between the official standard and the human-readable introductions, and you probably want both.

name: Assistant
mes:
Yes. The **official guide** is the **PDF specification itself**: **ISO 32000-2:2020 (PDF 2.0)**. The PDF Association hosts it **at no cost**, and its page explicitly says this is the latest and most up-to-date core PDF specification for developers who create, read, or process PDF files. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn457451view0Ņłü

For actual learning, I would not start with the full ISO spec cold unless you enjoy being hit with a brick made of standards prose. A better sequence is:

- **Start here for orientation:** the **PDF Association cheat sheets**. They are meant to help developers remember key PDF terms and concepts, but they explicitly say they are **not** a substitute for the core specification. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn938843search1Ņłü
- **Then read this for the classic developer-facing explanation:** **Adobe PDF Reference 1.7** in the Acrobat SDK docs. Adobe still lists the PDF Reference documents in its developer docs, and the 1.7 reference is the old, widely used guide people still point to for understanding PDF internals. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn938843search8Ņłéturn938843search31Ņłü
- **Then use the real standard:** **ISO 32000-2** for the authoritative version of how PDF is defined now. The PDF Association page also notes that PDF 2.0 includes corrections and clarifications that help even when supporting earlier PDF versions. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn457451view0Ņłü

If you want the shortest ŌĆ£where exactly do I go?ŌĆØ answer:

1. **PDF Association ŌåÆ ISO 32000-2** for the official spec. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn457451view0Ņłü  
2. **PDF Association ŌåÆ PDF Cheat Sheets** for a humane on-ramp. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn938843search1Ņłü  
3. **Adobe Acrobat SDK docs ŌåÆ PDF Reference 1.7** for the classic explanatory reference. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn938843search8Ņłéturn938843search31Ņłü

One more useful thing: the PDF Association page for ISO 32000-2 also points to **normative references** and a **technical index**, which is where you go once you start needing the surrounding standards and related documents. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn457451view0Ņłü

If you want, I can map the PDF spec to the OOXML mental model next ŌĆö like **Catalog Ōēł package root**, **Page tree**, **indirect objects**, **streams**, **xref**, and **content operators** ŌĆö so it becomes less alien.

name: You
mes: What javascript can extract and/or write at that level?
swipes: What javascript can extract and/or write at that level?

name: Assistant
mes: IŌĆÖm checking which JavaScript tools actually operate at the **object/content-stream level**, as opposed to normal app-level ŌĆ£merge, fill, renderŌĆØ libraries. That distinction matters a lot here because the ecosystem gets much thinner at the truly low level.
swipes: IŌĆÖm checking which JavaScript tools actually operate at the **object/content-stream level**, as opposed to normal app-level ŌĆ£merge, fill, renderŌĆØ libraries. That distinction matters a lot here because the ecosystem gets much thinner at the truly low level.

name: Assistant
mes:
## Yes, but the list gets much shorter

At the **object/content-stream level**, JavaScript has some real options, but not many. The strongest split is:

- **Read / inspect at low level**: **PDF.js**
- **Write / manipulate object graph in pure JS**: **pdf-lib**
- **Lower-level Node-side create/parse/manipulate with native bindings**: **MuhammaraJS**
- **Generate new PDFs with fairly low-level control**: **PDFKit** ŅłĆciteŅłéturn175901search3Ņłéturn842924search1Ņłéturn305842search3Ņłéturn842924search3Ņłü

## 1. PDF.js ŌĆö best low-level reader/inspector in JavaScript

If your goal is **extracting what the PDF is doing**, **PDF.js** is the main open-source JavaScript choice. Its API exposes:

- `getOperatorList()` for the pageŌĆÖs **operator list**
- `getTextContent()` for extracted text plus positioning/style info
- `getStructTree()` for page structure tree access
- `getMarkInfo()` for document MarkInfo
- `getData()` for raw PDF bytes
- `saveDocument()` for saved document bytes in the PDF.js editing flow ŅłĆciteŅłéturn175901search4Ņłü

That makes PDF.js the closest thing in JS to a **parser/viewer with access to page-level internals**. But its core identity is still a **reader/rendering platform**, not a general-purpose low-level writer. Mozilla describes it as a ŌĆ£general-purpose, web standards-based platform for parsing and rendering PDFs.ŌĆØ ŅłĆciteŅłéturn114434search1Ņłéturn175901search1Ņłü

### Good for
- parsing
- rendering
- inspecting text/layout/operators
- reading structure tree / marked content

### Not the best fit for
- arbitrary low-level PDF rewriting as your main write engine

## 2. pdf-lib ŌĆö best pure-JS write/manipulate option

For **writing** at something closer to the object-model layer, **pdf-lib** is the most important pure-JS option. Its public API exposes a document `catalog` and a low-level `context`, which is a strong hint that you can work below the friendly page/text/form layer when needed. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn842924search4Ņłü

This is the one I would reach for if you want:

- pure JS
- browser + Node + Deno portability
- object-level document manipulation
- creating/modifying PDFs without native dependencies ŅłĆciteŅłéturn114434search0Ņłéturn842924search3Ņłü

### Good for
- creating and modifying PDFs
- low-level-ish object access through `context`
- cross-runtime use

### Limits
It is not primarily documented as a **content-stream surgery toolkit** in the way a hardcore C/C++ PDF library would be. You can get lower than the friendly API, but that path is less ergonomic and less ŌĆ£officially tutorializedŌĆØ than the mainstream use cases. That last sentence is an inference from the docs and API surface, not a vendor statement. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn842924search4Ņłéturn114434search0Ņłü

## 3. MuhammaraJS ŌĆö closest to old-school low-level Node PDF hacking

If you are in **Node** and you want something more explicitly about **creating, parsing, and manipulating PDF files and streams**, **MuhammaraJS** is the most on-point answer I found. Its npm page describes it that way directly, and it is explicitly the maintained replacement for **HummusJS**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn305842search3Ņłéturn877228view0Ņłü

HummusJS itself says support ended in 2019 and recommends moving to MuhammaraJS. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn877228view0Ņłü

### Good for
- Node-only lower-level PDF work
- parsing/manipulating streams
- people who want something closer to ŌĆ£real PDF internalsŌĆØ than a browser library

### Limits
- native dependency stack, so less pleasant than pure JS
- not browser-first
- ecosystem/docs feel older than PDF.js/pdf-lib even if the replacement is still published ŅłĆciteŅłéturn305842search3Ņłéturn877228view0Ņłü

## 4. PDFKit ŌĆö low-level generation, not low-level parsing

**PDFKit** is more of a **writer/generator** than a parser, but it does matter here because its docs explicitly say it includes **low level functions** as well as higher-level abstractions. It also has unusually serious documentation for **Tagged PDF / PDF/UA** generation, including marked content, logical structure, and document options like `tagged: true` and `subset: 'PDF/UA'`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn842924search1Ņłéturn842924search0Ņłü

### Good for
- generating PDFs from scratch
- adding structure intentionally during generation
- accessible PDF generation in JS

### Not for
- deep extraction/parsing of arbitrary existing PDFs

## My blunt recommendation by job

### If you want to **extract/analyze page internals**
Use **PDF.js**. It is the strongest JS answer for reading operator lists, text content, structure trees, and similar internals. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn175901search4Ņłéturn114434search1Ņłü

### If you want to **write/manipulate at object level in pure JS**
Use **pdf-lib**. It is the cleanest mainstream choice with real low-level access points. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn842924search4Ņłéturn114434search0Ņłü

### If you want **Node-side lower-level PDF manipulation with streams/files**
Use **MuhammaraJS**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn305842search3Ņłéturn877228view0Ņłü

### If you want to **generate structured PDFs from scratch**
Use **PDFKit**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn842924search1Ņłéturn842924search0Ņłü

## The honest bottom line

If by ŌĆ£that levelŌĆØ you mean **the PDF object graph, page operators, marked content, structure tree, xref-ish internals**, JavaScript can do some of it, but the ecosystem is uneven:

- **PDF.js** is the best reader/inspector.
- **pdf-lib** is the best pure-JS writer/manipulator.
- **MuhammaraJS** is the more ŌĆ£PDF plumbingŌĆØ Node option.
- **PDFKit** is the best generation-first writer with meaningful structure support. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn175901search4Ņłéturn842924search4Ņłéturn305842search3Ņłéturn842924search0Ņłü

If you want, I can do the next layer as a **capability matrix** with rows like:

- read raw objects
- inspect content stream operators
- extract text positions
- inspect structure tree/tags
- create new indirect objects
- rewrite existing streams
- preserve accessibility structure
- browser vs Node only

- rewrite existing streams
- preserve accessibility structure
- browser vs Node only

name: Assistant
mes:
## A useful way to think about PDF accessibility An accessible PDF is **not one thing**. It is several layers that all have to line up well enough that a screen reader, keyboard user, low-vision user, mobile user, and sighted mouse user can all get the same document in usable form. The cleanest mental model is: 1. **Source document accessibility** 2. **Real text layer** 3. **Page content and visual layout** 4. **Semantic structure and tags** 5. **Reading order** 6. **Non-text alternatives** 7. **Tables, lists, headings, and links** 8. **Forms and interactive controls** 9. **Navigation aids** 10. **Document metadata and language** 11. **Security/settings that do not break assistive tech** 12. **Manual QA beyond automated checks** That is basically the whole game. The sources you uploaded are pretty aligned on this: start with an accessible source file, preserve accessibility on export, make sure the PDF has real text and tags, then check reading order, alt text, metadata, and forms. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü --- ## 1. Source document accessibility PDF is usually a **destination format**, not the best place to create accessibility from scratch. If the Word, PowerPoint, InDesign, or other source file is inaccessible, the PDF will usually inherit that damage, and fixing it later in Acrobat is much harder. The guidance you uploaded is explicit about this. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü This means the real first accessibility layer is: - proper heading structure in the source - real lists, not fake bullets - real tables, not spaced text - alt text in the source - good color contrast - descriptive links - language and title - sane reading order in the source layout --- ## 2. Real text layer If the PDF is just an **image of text**, it is inaccessible until OCR creates searchable/selectable text. Multiple uploaded sources say this directly. A scanned image is not enough because assistive tech cannot reliably read or extract the words from the image itself. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü So one core piece is: - **Does the PDF contain real machine-readable text?** That breaks down into: - selectable/searchable text exists - OCR accuracy is good enough - characters map correctly to Unicode - fonts are extractable to text, not just visually renderable AdobeŌĆÖs overview explicitly calls out searchable text and fonts that allow correct character extraction as accessibility characteristics. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü --- ## 3. Page content and visual layout This is the part people usually notice first because it is what appears on screen. WebAIM frames a PDF as having a **visual layer**, a **content layer**, and a **tags layer**. That is a very useful model. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file4Ņłü The visual/page layer includes: - text placement - columns - images - lines and shapes - headers/footers - page breaks - visual emphasis - color and contrast This layer matters because: - low-vision users may zoom or reflow - the visual order can differ from the semantic order - what looks obvious visually may be meaningless to a screen reader unless tagged properly --- ## 4. Semantic structure and tags This is one of the biggest pieces. A PDF needs a **tag tree / structure tree** so assistive technology can tell what something *is*: - heading - paragraph - list - list item - table - row - cell - figure - link - form field - artifact/decorative item Without tags, a PDF may still look perfect and still be functionally broken for nonvisual use. Harvard and Colorado both treat ŌĆ£no tagsŌĆØ as a major failure point, and Adobe describes tagged structure as what defines reading order and structural relationships. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü This is where PDF starts to behave less like a picture and more like a document. --- ## 5. Reading order A PDF can have all the right text and still be awful because the order is wrong. Reading order is the sequence assistive tech follows through the tagged content. Harvard explicitly says to inspect the order of tags in the Tags panel and verify that it matches the order the document should be read. Colorado also points out that automated tools do not reliably judge reading order quality. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü This piece includes: - correct order across columns - figures appearing in sensible places - sidebars inserted where they belong - repeated headers/footers treated appropriately - decorative items not interrupting content - form fields in logical sequence A visually pretty two-column PDF is one of the classic ways to destroy reading order. --- ## 6. Headings, lists, paragraphs, and document outline A document needs recognizable structure, not just styled text. That means: - one logical heading hierarchy - headings in proper nesting order - real list tags for lists - real paragraph tags for paragraphs - no fake structure made only with font size or bold text HarvardŌĆÖs examples call out expected tags like `

` through `

`, ``, `
  • `, ``, and `

    `. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü This matters because structure is how users: - skim - navigate - understand section relationships - jump around efficiently --- ## 7. Tables Tables are one of the most fragile parts of PDF accessibility. An accessible table needs more than grid lines. It needs structure that communicates: - this is a table - these are rows and columns - these are headers - these data cells belong under these headers If a table is visually present but semantically flattened into paragraphs or loose text objects, the information becomes much harder to interpret with assistive tech. Even when your uploaded pages do not go deeply into table repair syntax, they repeatedly emphasize that data tables are part of the structural elements that should be defined in the source and preserved in the PDF. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü --- ## 8. Images, figures, charts, and other non-text content Non-text content needs a usable text alternative. That means: - meaningful images get alt text - decorative images are marked decorative/artifacted appropriately - charts/diagrams get enough description to preserve the informational value - purely visual meaning is not left unexplained Harvard says images should be tagged as `

name: You
mes: Right. And IŌĆÖm asking you to think about available JavaScript with all those layers
swipes: Right. And IŌĆÖm asking you to think about available JavaScript with all those layers

name: Assistant
mes: IŌĆÖm mapping the JavaScript ecosystem against the actual accessibility layers now: text/OCR, structure tags, reading order, metadata, forms, navigation, and remediation. The likely answer is uneven rather than clean.
swipes: IŌĆÖm mapping the JavaScript ecosystem against the actual accessibility layers now: text/OCR, structure tags, reading order, metadata, forms, navigation, and remediation. The likely answer is uneven rather than clean.

name: Assistant
mes:
## The right conclusion

If you think about **all** the accessibility layers at once, the JavaScript story is not ŌĆ£thereŌĆÖs one PDF library.ŌĆØ It is:

- **PDF.js** for **inspection and parsing**
- **PDFKit** for **authoring new accessible PDFs from scratch**
- **pdf-lib** for **basic editing, forms, and metadata**
- **Scribe.js** or **Tesseract.js + rendering** for **OCR/searchable text**
- **Acrobat/manual remediation** for the hard parts that none of the JS tools clearly own end to end

That last point is not me being dramatic. Your uploaded guidance is already clear that PDF accessibility is complex, that accessible source documents matter, that export must preserve accessibility, and that remediation inside Acrobat is specialized work. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü

## Layer by layer

### 1. Source-layer accessibility

If you control the source content, JavaScript is in a much better position. The accessibility guidance you uploaded is consistent that accessibility should start in the source document and be preserved on export, because fixing a bad PDF later is much harder. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü

So for JS, the healthiest use case is:

- generate a PDF from **structured, already-accessible content**
- not ŌĆ£take a random broken PDF and magically fix all semantics afterwardŌĆØ

That is exactly where **PDFKit** fits best, because it explicitly supports accessibility features including **marked content, logical structure, Tagged PDF, and PDF/UA**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view1Ņłéturn757366view0Ņłü

### 2. Real text / OCR / searchable text

For the text layer, JS has workable options, but they split into two different jobs:

- **extract existing text from text-native PDFs**
- **OCR image-based PDFs**

Your uploaded sources are explicit that image-only PDFs are inaccessible until OCR creates real searchable text, and that OCR output often still needs more work. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü

In JS:

- **PDF.js** can extract text content from existing PDFs with `getTextContent()` and can include marked content items as well. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn481455search1Ņłü
- **Tesseract.js** is a JS OCR engine, but its own docs say it **does not support PDF files directly**; you must render PDFs to images first or use something else. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn809246search1Ņłéturn809246search3Ņłü
- **Scribe.js** is more interesting for PDF work specifically because its README says it can extract text from text-native PDFs, OCR image-native PDFs, and write PDFs with an invisible text layer to make them searchable. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn473311search1Ņłéturn473311search3Ņłü

That means JS can cover the **searchable text layer** reasonably well, especially with **PDF.js + Scribe.js**. It does **not** mean JS has solved the semantic layer.

### 3. Visual/page-content layer

If you mean the literal page description layer ŌĆö drawn text, shapes, images, content streams, operator sequences ŌĆö JS is actually pretty good at **reading** it and decent at **writing** it.

- **PDF.js** exposes page-level internals such as `getOperatorList()` and `getTextContent()`. Its own docs describe a core layer where the binary PDF is parsed and interpreted, though they warn that using the core layer directly is advanced and unstable. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn755860view0Ņłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn481455search1Ņłü
- **PDFKit** is explicitly a generation library with both lower-level functions and higher-level abstractions. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view1Ņłü
- **pdf-lib** exposes a `catalog` and a low-level `context`, which tells you it can work below just the ŌĆ£draw some textŌĆØ surface. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn432113search1Ņłü

So for the raw page-content layer, JS is fine.

### 4. Semantic structure and tags

This is where the field narrows hard.

The semantic/tag layer is the one your uploaded docs keep treating as essential: headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, figures, links, and other content need meaningful tags and structure, and ŌĆ£no tagsŌĆØ is a major failure mode. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü

For JS:

- **PDF.js** can **read** the page structure tree with `getStructTree()`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn481455search1Ņłü
- **PDFKit** can **author** marked content and build a logical structure tree, and its accessibility docs are unusually explicit about Tagged PDF and PDF/UA authoring. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłü
- **pdf-lib** does not advertise comparable tag-tree authoring or structure-tree APIs in its public docs that I found; its documented accessibility-adjacent pieces are mainly metadata and forms, not Tagged PDF authoring. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn501032view1Ņłéturn501032view2Ņłü

So the JS ecosystem has a real split:

- **read existing structure**: yes, with PDF.js
- **author new structure from scratch**: yes, with PDFKit
- **robustly edit existing arbitrary tag trees**: I did **not** find a mature, mainstream JS library that clearly documents this as a strength

That last sentence is a searched negative result, not a universal impossibility claim.

### 5. Reading order

Reading order is one of the places where PDF accessibility stops being a ŌĆ£library featureŌĆØ and becomes a ŌĆ£document truthŌĆØ problem. Your uploaded guidance emphasizes that reading order has to be checked and that automated tools do not judge it reliably. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü

JS can help here, but mostly in two ways:

- **inspect** the current structure/order with **PDF.js**
- **author in the correct order from the start** with **PDFKit**

PDFKitŌĆÖs accessibility docs are actually strong on this point: they explicitly say to write objects in the natural reading order and to add logical structure with all significant content included. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłü

What JS is **not** especially strong at is taking a messy existing PDF and giving you a comfortable, mainstream remediation API for reordering the semantic reading sequence the way Acrobat specialists do.

### 6. Headings, lists, paragraphs, and tables

For **new authoring**, **PDFKit** is the clear best JS story I found.

Its accessibility docs document standard structure element types including headings, paragraphs, lists, list bodies, tables, rows, heading cells, data cells, figures, links, and form widgets. It also has table support, and its table docs include a cell `type` option ŌĆ£for accessibilityŌĆØ with default `TD`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłéturn969554view2Ņłü

That means if you are generating a report, handout, or structured document from data, JS can do a serious amount here.

Where it gets weaker is **retrofitting** that structure onto an existing arbitrary PDF.

### 7. Images, figures, alt text, actual text, artifacts

Again, **PDFKit** is the standout for new authoring. Its accessibility docs support structure element options like `alt`, `actual`, `expanded`, and language overrides, and they explicitly require marking non-structure content as `Artifact`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłü

That maps very closely to the non-text-content requirements in your uploaded guidance: meaningful figures need useful alternative text, decorative content should not pollute the reading flow, and structure needs to represent what matters. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü

For **inspection**, PDF.js can help you see whether the structure tree exists, but it is not a remediation authoring system. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn755860view0Ņłü

### 8. Links, destinations, bookmarks, navigation

JS can do this better than it can do tag remediation.

- **PDFKit** supports **outlines/bookmarks** and **named destinations/go-to links**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn969554view0Ņłéturn969554view1Ņłü
- **PDF.js** can inspect annotations and actions. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn481455search1Ņłü
- **pdf-lib** can manipulate PDFs and forms, but its own docs note that `copy()` will not copy all information such as **acroforms and outlines**, and there are issue reports about links being lost after combining PDFs. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn571291view3Ņłéturn950417search6Ņłü

So JS can author navigation decently, but **preserving** navigation/interactivity during transformations is less reassuring, especially in generic editing flows.

### 9. Forms and interactive controls

This layer is mixed.

- **pdf-lib** clearly supports working with forms through `getForm()`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn571291view1Ņłü
- **PDFKit** has explicit AcroForm support and form annotation methods like `formText`, `formCombo`, `formList`, and push buttons. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view1Ņłéturn417269view0Ņłü

But the accessibility story is not clean:

- PDFKitŌĆÖs accessibility docs say it currently supports only **marked content** in the logical structure, **not annotations, forms, or anything else**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłü
- PDFKitŌĆÖs form docs warn that `NeedAppearances` may not be honored by all viewers, that document JavaScript is not widely supported outside Acrobat, and that radio buttons and checkboxes are not supported in that initial forms release. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn417269view0Ņłéturn417269view1Ņłéturn417269view2Ņłéturn417269view3Ņłü
- pdf-lib has issue reports about filled fields rendering inconsistently in Acrobat after page-copy/merge workflows. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn950417search5Ņłéturn950417search8Ņłü

So JS can **create and fill forms**, but ŌĆ£fully accessible, robust across viewers, semantically integrated into the structure treeŌĆØ is still shaky.

### 10. Metadata and document properties

This layer is easy in JS.

**pdf-lib** has documented support for setting:

- title
- window-title display behavior
- language
- author
- subject
- keywords
- creation/modification dates
- producer/creator metadata ŅłĆciteŅłéturn571291view0Ņłéturn571291view2Ņłéturn501032view2Ņłü

That aligns well with the uploaded guidance around title, language, and document properties. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü

So for the metadata/control layer, JS is fine.

### 11. Security and permissions

This is not the strongest accessibility layer for JS, but there is some support.

**PDFKit** documents security features and access privileges including content accessibility. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view1Ņłü

But if your goal is subtle accessibility-safe security handling across many viewer combinations, I would still be cautious. Your uploaded sources emphasize that security should not interfere with assistive technology, and this is another area where final QA matters more than API surface. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü

## So what does JavaScript actually cover well?

### Strong in JS
- **Inspect existing PDFs**: PDF.js ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn755860view0Ņłü
- **Extract text / annotations / structure tree info**: PDF.js; Scribe.js for OCR/text extraction paths ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn473311search1Ņłéturn473311search3Ņłü
- **Generate new structured PDFs**: PDFKit ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłéturn757366view1Ņłü
- **Set metadata and basic form data**: pdf-lib, PDFKit ŅłĆciteŅłéturn571291view1Ņłéturn571291view2Ņłéturn417269view0Ņłü
- **Add bookmarks/destinations/navigation**: PDFKit ŅłĆciteŅłéturn969554view0Ņłéturn969554view1Ņłü

### Weak or incomplete in JS
- **Full remediation of existing arbitrary inaccessible PDFs**
- **Comfortable editing of existing tag trees / reading order**
- **Reliable preservation of all interactive/accessibility features through merge/copy flows**
- **Fully robust accessible forms across viewers**
- **One-library coverage of all layers at once** ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłéturn571291view3Ņłéturn950417search5Ņłéturn950417search6Ņłü

## My actual recommendation

If you want to use **JavaScript while respecting all the accessibility layers**, the best pattern is:

1. **Author from structured accessible source data**
2. **Generate the PDF with PDFKit** when you need a new PDF
3. **Inspect with PDF.js** when you need to audit an existing PDF
4. **Use Scribe.js** when you need OCR/searchable text in a JS stack
5. **Use pdf-lib for metadata/basic form/basic manipulation**
6. **Do not expect a JS-only stack to replace Acrobat-style remediation for arbitrary broken PDFs**

That recommendation follows both the current JS tooling reality and the accessibility guidance in your uploaded sources. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn473311search3Ņłü

## The one-sentence version

JavaScript can cover **most individual layers** of PDF accessibility, but it does **not** currently give you a clean, mature, mainstream **end-to-end remediation stack** for **all** layers in messy real-world PDFs.

I can turn this into a **layer-by-layer capability matrix** next, with columns like **PDF.js / PDFKit / pdf-lib / Scribe.js / Acrobat fallback / confidence level**.

- **extract existing text from text-native PDFs**
- **OCR image-based PDFs**

Your uploaded sources are explicit that image-only PDFs are inaccessible until OCR creates real searchable text, and that OCR output often still needs more work. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü

In JS:

- **PDF.js** can extract text content from existing PDFs with `getTextContent()` and can include marked content items as well. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn481455search1Ņłü
- **Tesseract.js** is a JS OCR engine, but its own docs say it **does not support PDF files directly**; you must render PDFs to images first or use something else. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn809246search1Ņłéturn809246search3Ņłü
- **Scribe.js** is more interesting for PDF work specifically because its README says it can extract text from text-native PDFs, OCR image-native PDFs, and write PDFs with an invisible text layer to make them searchable. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn473311search1Ņłéturn473311search3Ņłü

That means JS can cover the **searchable text layer** reasonably well, especially with **PDF.js + Scribe.js**. It does **not** mean JS has solved the semantic layer.

### 3. Visual/page-content layer

If you mean the literal page description layer ŌĆö drawn text, shapes, images, content streams, operator sequences ŌĆö JS is actually pretty good at **reading** it and decent at **writing** it.

- **PDF.js** exposes page-level internals such as `getOperatorList()` and `getTextContent()`. Its own docs describe a core layer where the binary PDF is parsed and interpreted, though they warn that using the core layer directly is advanced and unstable. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn755860view0Ņłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn481455search1Ņłü
- **PDFKit** is explicitly a generation library with both lower-level functions and higher-level abstractions. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view1Ņłü
- **pdf-lib** exposes a `catalog` and a low-level `context`, which tells you it can work below just the ŌĆ£draw some textŌĆØ surface. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn432113search1Ņłü

So for the raw page-content layer, JS is fine.

### 4. Semantic structure and tags

This is where the field narrows hard.

The semantic/tag layer is the one your uploaded docs keep treating as essential: headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, figures, links, and other content need meaningful tags and structure, and ŌĆ£no tagsŌĆØ is a major failure mode. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü

For JS:

- **PDF.js** can **read** the page structure tree with `getStructTree()`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn481455search1Ņłü
- **PDFKit** can **author** marked content and build a logical structure tree, and its accessibility docs are unusually explicit about Tagged PDF and PDF/UA authoring. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłü
- **pdf-lib** does not advertise comparable tag-tree authoring or structure-tree APIs in its public docs that I found; its documented accessibility-adjacent pieces are mainly metadata and forms, not Tagged PDF authoring. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn501032view1Ņłéturn501032view2Ņłü

So the JS ecosystem has a real split:

- **read existing structure**: yes, with PDF.js
- **author new structure from scratch**: yes, with PDFKit
- **robustly edit existing arbitrary tag trees**: I did **not** find a mature, mainstream JS library that clearly documents this as a strength

That last sentence is a searched negative result, not a universal impossibility claim.

### 5. Reading order

Reading order is one of the places where PDF accessibility stops being a ŌĆ£library featureŌĆØ and becomes a ŌĆ£document truthŌĆØ problem. Your uploaded guidance emphasizes that reading order has to be checked and that automated tools do not judge it reliably. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü

JS can help here, but mostly in two ways:

- **inspect** the current structure/order with **PDF.js**
- **author in the correct order from the start** with **PDFKit**

PDFKitŌĆÖs accessibility docs are actually strong on this point: they explicitly say to write objects in the natural reading order and to add logical structure with all significant content included. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłü

What JS is **not** especially strong at is taking a messy existing PDF and giving you a comfortable, mainstream remediation API for reordering the semantic reading sequence the way Acrobat specialists do.

### 6. Headings, lists, paragraphs, and tables

For **new authoring**, **PDFKit** is the clear best JS story I found.

Its accessibility docs document standard structure element types including headings, paragraphs, lists, list bodies, tables, rows, heading cells, data cells, figures, links, and form widgets. It also has table support, and its table docs include a cell `type` option ŌĆ£for accessibilityŌĆØ with default `TD`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłéturn969554view2Ņłü

That means if you are generating a report, handout, or structured document from data, JS can do a serious amount here.

Where it gets weaker is **retrofitting** that structure onto an existing arbitrary PDF.

### 7. Images, figures, alt text, actual text, artifacts

Again, **PDFKit** is the standout for new authoring. Its accessibility docs support structure element options like `alt`, `actual`, `expanded`, and language overrides, and they explicitly require marking non-structure content as `Artifact`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłü

That maps very closely to the non-text-content requirements in your uploaded guidance: meaningful figures need useful alternative text, decorative content should not pollute the reading flow, and structure needs to represent what matters. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü

For **inspection**, PDF.js can help you see whether the structure tree exists, but it is not a remediation authoring system. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn755860view0Ņłü

### 8. Links, destinations, bookmarks, navigation

JS can do this better than it can do tag remediation.

- **PDFKit** supports **outlines/bookmarks** and **named destinations/go-to links**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn969554view0Ņłéturn969554view1Ņłü
- **PDF.js** can inspect annotations and actions. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn481455search1Ņłü
- **pdf-lib** can manipulate PDFs and forms, but its own docs note that `copy()` will not copy all information such as **acroforms and outlines**, and there are issue reports about links being lost after combining PDFs. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn571291view3Ņłéturn950417search6Ņłü

So JS can author navigation decently, but **preserving** navigation/interactivity during transformations is less reassuring, especially in generic editing flows.

### 9. Forms and interactive controls

This layer is mixed.

- **pdf-lib** clearly supports working with forms through `getForm()`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn571291view1Ņłü
- **PDFKit** has explicit AcroForm support and form annotation methods like `formText`, `formCombo`, `formList`, and push buttons. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view1Ņłéturn417269view0Ņłü

But the accessibility story is not clean:

- PDFKitŌĆÖs accessibility docs say it currently supports only **marked content** in the logical structure, **not annotations, forms, or anything else**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłü
- PDFKitŌĆÖs form docs warn that `NeedAppearances` may not be honored by all viewers, that document JavaScript is not widely supported outside Acrobat, and that radio buttons and checkboxes are not supported in that initial forms release. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn417269view0Ņłéturn417269view1Ņłéturn417269view2Ņłéturn417269view3Ņłü
- pdf-lib has issue reports about filled fields rendering inconsistently in Acrobat after page-copy/merge workflows. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn950417search5Ņłéturn950417search8Ņłü

So JS can **create and fill forms**, but ŌĆ£fully accessible, robust across viewers, semantically integrated into the structure treeŌĆØ is still shaky.

### 10. Metadata and document properties

This layer is easy in JS.

**pdf-lib** has documented support for setting:

- title
- window-title display behavior
- language
- author
- subject
- keywords
- creation/modification dates
- producer/creator metadata ŅłĆciteŅłéturn571291view0Ņłéturn571291view2Ņłéturn501032view2Ņłü

That aligns well with the uploaded guidance around title, language, and document properties. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü

So for the metadata/control layer, JS is fine.

### 11. Security and permissions

This is not the strongest accessibility layer for JS, but there is some support.

**PDFKit** documents security features and access privileges including content accessibility. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view1Ņłü

But if your goal is subtle accessibility-safe security handling across many viewer combinations, I would still be cautious. Your uploaded sources emphasize that security should not interfere with assistive technology, and this is another area where final QA matters more than API surface. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file2Ņłü

## So what does JavaScript actually cover well?

### Strong in JS
- **Inspect existing PDFs**: PDF.js ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn755860view0Ņłü
- **Extract text / annotations / structure tree info**: PDF.js; Scribe.js for OCR/text extraction paths ŅłĆciteŅłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn473311search1Ņłéturn473311search3Ņłü
- **Generate new structured PDFs**: PDFKit ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłéturn757366view1Ņłü
- **Set metadata and basic form data**: pdf-lib, PDFKit ŅłĆciteŅłéturn571291view1Ņłéturn571291view2Ņłéturn417269view0Ņłü
- **Add bookmarks/destinations/navigation**: PDFKit ŅłĆciteŅłéturn969554view0Ņłéturn969554view1Ņłü

### Weak or incomplete in JS
- **Full remediation of existing arbitrary inaccessible PDFs**
- **Comfortable editing of existing tag trees / reading order**
- **Reliable preservation of all interactive/accessibility features through merge/copy flows**
- **Fully robust accessible forms across viewers**
- **One-library coverage of all layers at once** ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłéturn571291view3Ņłéturn950417search5Ņłéturn950417search6Ņłü

## My actual recommendation

If you want to use **JavaScript while respecting all the accessibility layers**, the best pattern is:

1. **Author from structured accessible source data**
2. **Generate the PDF with PDFKit** when you need a new PDF
3. **Inspect with PDF.js** when you need to audit an existing PDF
4. **Use Scribe.js** when you need OCR/searchable text in a JS stack
5. **Use pdf-lib for metadata/basic form/basic manipulation**
6. **Do not expect a JS-only stack to replace Acrobat-style remediation for arbitrary broken PDFs**

That recommendation follows both the current JS tooling reality and the accessibility guidance in your uploaded sources. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn0file3Ņłü ŅłĆciteŅłéturn757366view0Ņłéturn481455search0Ņłéturn473311search3Ņłü

## The one-sentence version

JavaScript can cover **most individual layers** of PDF accessibility, but it does **not** currently give you a clean, mature, mainstream **end-to-end remediation stack** for **all** layers in messy real-world PDFs.

I can turn this into a **layer-by-layer capability matrix** next, with columns like **PDF.js / PDFKit / pdf-lib / Scribe.js / Acrobat fallback / confidence level**.

## No-Adobe JavaScript map

Using your docs as the **requirements baseline**, the layers that matter are: real text/OCR, tags/structure, reading order, headings/lists/tables/figures, alt text/artifacts, forms, navigation, metadata/language, permissions, and QA.

Assuming **Adobe is off the table**, here is the side-by-side reality.

## Open-source JavaScript

| Tool | Runtime | Inspect existing PDFs | OCR / searchable text | Create new accessible/tagged PDFs | Edit existing tags / reading order | Forms / nav / metadata | Best fit | Biggest limitation | Evidence |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|---|
| **PDF.js** | Browser first; JS display/viewer stack | **Strong** | **No OCR** | **No** | **Inspect only** | **Inspect only** | Audit existing PDFs, build viewers, extract text/layout/structure | Not a real remediation/authoring engine | PDF.js documents a **Core** layer for parsing/interpreting PDFs, a **Display** layer for rendering and extracting info, and APIs including `getTextContent()`, `getOperatorList()`, `getStructTree()`, `getAnnotations()`, `getMarkInfo()`, `getMetadata()`, `getFieldObjects()`, `getPermissions()`, and `saveDocument()`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn536193search5Ņłéturn536193search0Ņłéturn536193search2Ņłéturn536193search1Ņłü |
| **Scribe.js** | Browser + Node | **Moderate** | **Strong** | **Text layer only** | **No** | **No meaningful accessibility semantics** | Make image PDFs searchable; OCR mixed collections; extract text from PDFs | Gives you **searchable text**, not full semantic remediation | Scribe.js says it can extract existing text from text-native PDFs, OCR image-native PDFs, and write PDFs with an **invisible text layer** to make them searchable. Tesseract.js itself says it does **not** support PDFs directly and points people to Scribe.js or a render-to-images workflow. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn255725search1Ņłéturn459194search2Ņłü |
| **PDFKit** | Node | **No** | **No** | **Strongest open-source option** | **No, generation-first** | **Good** | Generate new accessibility-aware PDFs from structured data you control | Its logical-structure support currently covers **marked content only**, not annotations/forms/other objects in the structure tree | PDFKit documents Tagged PDF / **PDF/UA** creation with `tagged: true`, `subset: 'PDF/UA'`, title/language, logical structure, alt/actual text, artifacts, and natural reading order. It documents standard structure types for headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, figures, and links. It also documents **forms**, **bookmarks/outlines**, **named destinations**, and annotation helpers. It also explicitly says: ŌĆ£So far, PDFKit only supports marked content in the logical structure, not annotations, forms, or anything else.ŌĆØ ŅłĆciteŅłéturn749935search2Ņłéturn749935search3Ņłéturn749935search0Ņłéturn749935search1Ņłéturn749935search4Ņłü |
| **pdf-lib** | Browser + Node + Deno | **Moderate** | **No OCR** | **Basic PDF creation/editing** | **Not documented as a tag/remediation tool** | **Good for metadata and AcroForms** | Fill forms, set metadata, make basic edits, assemble docs | Weak fit for accessibility-preserving transforms; documented copy omissions and issue history around links/forms/outlines | pdf-lib documents low-level access via `context` and `catalog`, plus metadata APIs like `setTitle`, `setLanguage`, etc., and `PDFForm`/AcroForm support. But its own docs say `copy()` will **not** copy all information, including **acroforms** and **outlines**. Its issue tracker shows reports of outlines being overwritten, links being lost after combining PDFs, and multi-page filled fields not rendering consistently in some viewers. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn255725search3Ņłéturn255725search2Ņłéturn749935search5Ņłéturn748355search3Ņłéturn748355search1Ņłéturn748355search5Ņłéturn748355search6Ņłü |
| **MuhammaraJS** | Node | **Low-level PDF plumbing** | **No built-in OCR evidence** | **Low-level create/manipulate** | **No documented accessibility stack** | **Low-level only** | Stream/object-level PDF manipulation in Node | I did not find first-party docs showing a serious accessibility/tagging/PDF/UA workflow | MuhammaraJS describes itself as a fast Node module for **creating, parsing and manipulating PDF files and streams**, and as a replacement for HummusJS. The public docs I found are about low-level PDF manipulation, not accessibility remediation. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn459194search1Ņłéturn459194search5Ņłü |

## Commercial no-Adobe JavaScript SDKs

| Tool | Runtime | Inspect existing PDFs | OCR / searchable text | Create / edit tags & reading order | Accessible viewing experience | Forms / editing / validation | Best fit | Biggest limitation | Evidence |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|---|
| **Apryse WebViewer / Apryse SDK** | Browser JS SDK | **Strong** | Not the main point in the pages I checked | **Closest documented no-Adobe remediation story I found** | **Strong** | **Strong** | Teams that need a JS-accessible viewer plus documented tag/PDF/UA tooling | Commercial; some accessibility features depend on the PDF already being properly tagged | Apryse documents **Accessible Reading Order Mode** for tagged PDFs in WebViewer and says it inserts document structure into the DOM based on PDF tags. Separately, Apryse support docs describe **PDF/UA validation**, **create/edit document tags**, **auto-tag PDF**, a **Tags Explorer**, and creating content tags. Their product pages also document form creation, text editing, and a WCAG-compliant UI. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn417379search6Ņłéturn417379search8Ņłéturn417379search1Ņłéturn417379search7Ņłü |
| **Nutrient Web SDK** | Browser JS SDK | **Strong** | Not the main point in the pages I checked | **I did not find equivalent tag-edit/remediation claims in the pages I checked** | **Very strong** | **Strong viewer/editor/forms** | High-quality accessible viewing and app integration when tagged PDFs already exist | Based on the docs I checked, its strongest documented accessibility story is **viewer/UI and HTML rendering of tagged PDFs**, not full tag remediation | Nutrient documents screen-reader-friendly UI, keyboard support, localized accessibility strings, and rendering **tagged PDF / PDF/UA** as **WCAG 2.1-accessible HTML**. It also documents a broad web SDK for viewing, editing, annotations, and forms. I did not find, in the pages I checked, the same kind of explicit tag-tree creation/edit/PDF-UA-validation remediation docs that Apryse publishes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn417379search0Ņłéturn417379search3Ņłéturn417379search4Ņłéturn417379search10Ņłéturn417379search2Ņłü |

## What this means in plain English

### 1. Best open-source tool for **auditing existing PDFs**
**PDF.js**

Why:
- It can inspect text, operators, annotations, structure tree, mark info, metadata, permissions, and form field objects. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn536193search0Ņłéturn536193search2Ņłéturn536193search1Ņłü

### 2. Best open-source tool for **making scanned PDFs searchable**
**Scribe.js**

Why:
- It can OCR image PDFs and write an invisible text layer back into PDFs. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn255725search1Ņłéturn459194search2Ņłü

### 3. Best open-source tool for **creating new accessibility-aware PDFs from scratch**
**PDFKit**

Why:
- It is the only open-source JS option I found with explicit first-party docs for Tagged PDF / PDF/UA authoring, structure trees, alt text, artifacts, reading order, headings/lists/tables, and document language/title. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn749935search2Ņłü

### 4. Best open-source tool for **basic edits, forms, and metadata**
**pdf-lib**

Why:
- It is good for metadata and AcroForms, and it exposes low-level document access.  
Why not for full accessibility remediation:
- Its own docs and issues make it a bad bet for preserving all higher-level structure during complex transforms. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn255725search3Ņłéturn749935search5Ņłéturn748355search3Ņłéturn748355search5Ņłéturn748355search6Ņłü

### 5. Best **no-Adobe commercial** JS option when you need **real remediation features**
**Apryse**, based on the documentation I found.

Why:
- It is the strongest documented non-Adobe JS-adjacent story I found for **tag editing, auto-tagging, tags explorer, and PDF/UA validation**, plus accessible reading order in the web viewer. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn417379search6Ņłéturn417379search8Ņłü

## The blunt conclusion

If you refuse Adobe and want **open-source JavaScript only**, the most realistic stack is:

- **PDF.js** for inspection/audit
- **Scribe.js** for OCR/searchable text
- **PDFKit** for generating new accessible PDFs from structured source data
- **pdf-lib** for metadata/forms/basic edits

That stack can cover **a lot**, but it does **not** give you a clean open-source JS answer for **full remediation of arbitrary broken PDFs across every accessibility layer**. The closest documented no-Adobe answer I found for that heavier remediation space is **Apryse**, not an open-source library. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn536193search5Ņłéturn255725search1Ņłéturn749935search2Ņłéturn748355search3Ņłéturn417379search8Ņłü

## My recommendation by scenario

| Scenario | Best no-Adobe JS choice |
|---|---|
| Audit existing PDFs for text/tags/forms/metadata/permissions | **PDF.js** |
| OCR scanned PDFs and make them searchable | **Scribe.js** |
| Generate brand-new accessible PDFs from structured app data | **PDFKit** |
| Fill forms and set title/language/metadata | **pdf-lib** |
| Need browser SDK with documented tag remediation / PDF-UA tooling | **Apryse** |
| Need highly accessible viewer experience for already-tagged PDFs | **Nutrient** or **Apryse** |

The cleanest open-source architecture is **PDF.js + Scribe.js + PDFKit**, with **pdf-lib** kept for limited edit/fill/metadata tasks.

|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|---|
| **Apryse WebViewer / Apryse SDK** | Browser JS SDK | **Strong** | Not the main point in the pages I checked | **Closest documented no-Adobe remediation story I found** | **Strong** | **Strong** | Teams that need a JS-accessible viewer plus documented tag/PDF/UA tooling | Commercial; some accessibility features depend on the PDF already being properly tagged | Apryse documents **Accessible Reading Order Mode** for tagged PDFs in WebViewer and says it inserts document structure into the DOM based on PDF tags. Separately, Apryse support docs describe **PDF/UA validation**, **create/edit document tags**, **auto-tag PDF**, a **Tags Explorer**, and creating content tags. Their product pages also document form creation, text editing, and a WCAG-compliant UI. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn417379search6Ņłéturn417379search8Ņłéturn417379search1Ņłéturn417379search7Ņłü |
| **Nutrient Web SDK** | Browser JS SDK | **Strong** | Not the main point in the pages I checked | **I did not find equivalent tag-edit/remediation claims in the pages I checked** | **Very strong** | **Strong viewer/editor/forms** | High-quality accessible viewing and app integration when tagged PDFs already exist | Based on the docs I checked, its strongest documented accessibility story is **viewer/UI and HTML rendering of tagged PDFs**, not full tag remediation | Nutrient documents screen-reader-friendly UI, keyboard support, localized accessibility strings, and rendering **tagged PDF / PDF/UA** as **WCAG 2.1-accessible HTML**. It also documents a broad web SDK for viewing, editing, annotations, and forms. I did not find, in the pages I checked, the same kind of explicit tag-tree creation/edit/PDF-UA-validation remediation docs that Apryse publishes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn417379search0Ņłéturn417379search3Ņłéturn417379search4Ņłéturn417379search10Ņłéturn417379search2Ņłü |

## What this means in plain English

### 1. Best open-source tool for **auditing existing PDFs**
**PDF.js**

Why:
- It can inspect text, operators, annotations, structure tree, mark info, metadata, permissions, and form field objects. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn536193search0Ņłéturn536193search2Ņłéturn536193search1Ņłü

### 2. Best open-source tool for **making scanned PDFs searchable**
**Scribe.js**

Why:
- It can OCR image PDFs and write an invisible text layer back into PDFs. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn255725search1Ņłéturn459194search2Ņłü

### 3. Best open-source tool for **creating new accessibility-aware PDFs from scratch**
**PDFKit**

Why:
- It is the only open-source JS option I found with explicit first-party docs for Tagged PDF / PDF/UA authoring, structure trees, alt text, artifacts, reading order, headings/lists/tables, and document language/title. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn749935search2Ņłü

### 4. Best open-source tool for **basic edits, forms, and metadata**
**pdf-lib**

Why:
- It is good for metadata and AcroForms, and it exposes low-level document access.  
Why not for full accessibility remediation:
- Its own docs and issues make it a bad bet for preserving all higher-level structure during complex transforms. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn255725search3Ņłéturn749935search5Ņłéturn748355search3Ņłéturn748355search5Ņłéturn748355search6Ņłü

### 5. Best **no-Adobe commercial** JS option when you need **real remediation features**
**Apryse**, based on the documentation I found.

Why:
- It is the strongest documented non-Adobe JS-adjacent story I found for **tag editing, auto-tagging, tags explorer, and PDF/UA validation**, plus accessible reading order in the web viewer. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn417379search6Ņłéturn417379search8Ņłü

## The blunt conclusion

If you refuse Adobe and want **open-source JavaScript only**, the most realistic stack is:

- **PDF.js** for inspection/audit
- **Scribe.js** for OCR/searchable text
- **PDFKit** for generating new accessible PDFs from structured source data
- **pdf-lib** for metadata/forms/basic edits

That stack can cover **a lot**, but it does **not** give you a clean open-source JS answer for **full remediation of arbitrary broken PDFs across every accessibility layer**. The closest documented no-Adobe answer I found for that heavier remediation space is **Apryse**, not an open-source library. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn536193search5Ņłéturn255725search1Ņłéturn749935search2Ņłéturn748355search3Ņłéturn417379search8Ņłü

## My recommendation by scenario

| Scenario | Best no-Adobe JS choice |
|---|---|
| Audit existing PDFs for text/tags/forms/metadata/permissions | **PDF.js** |
| OCR scanned PDFs and make them searchable | **Scribe.js** |
| Generate brand-new accessible PDFs from structured app data | **PDFKit** |
| Fill forms and set title/language/metadata | **pdf-lib** |
| Need browser SDK with documented tag remediation / PDF-UA tooling | **Apryse** |
| Need highly accessible viewer experience for already-tagged PDFs | **Nutrient** or **Apryse** |

The cleanest open-source architecture is **PDF.js + Scribe.js + PDFKit**, with **pdf-lib** kept for limited edit/fill/metadata tasks.
