name: Assistant
mes:
## The logic behind it

This schema is not trying to be a full semantic model of the deck. It is a **workflow manifest for a controlled conversion pipeline**:

**PPTX source ŌåÆ extracted/interpreted slide content ŌåÆ review flags ŌåÆ projected DOCX companion**

You can see that logic directly in the top-level structure:

- `source_package` describes the input as an OOXML/OPC/PresentationML package
- `target_package` describes the output as an OOXML/OPC/WordprocessingML package
- `run_config` records conversion choices like preview images and `PAGE_BREAK`
- `slide_entries` is the actual per-slide conversion record ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

So the manifest is basically a **receipt plus decision log** for how a slide deck is being turned into a Word companion document.

## Why PPTX to DOCX specifically

The design assumption is:

- **PPTX is the visual source**
- **DOCX is the editable accessibility-review target**

That is why every slide has a `projected_target` object with fields like:

- `projected_slide_heading_text`
- `projected_slide_heading_style`
- `projected_slide_separator_mode`
- `projection_status`

The pipeline is saying: ŌĆ£For this slide, here is what will become the corresponding Word heading and where the break goes.ŌĆØ In other words, each slide is being turned into a **Word section/chunk** rather than kept as a slide-native visual object. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

## The core structural idea

Each slide entry is split into **three layers**:

### 1. `observed_source`
What the converter literally found in the PPTX.

Examples:
- which slide XML part it came from
- whether a text block was a `placeholder_text`, `standalone_text_box`, or `table_cell`
- the original PowerPoint shape name
- preview image candidates ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

This is the **raw evidence layer**.

### 2. `normalized_workflow`
What the converter thinks that source element probably *means* for the workflow.

Examples:
- `chosen_title`
- `title_source`
- `interpreted_text_role`
- `role_confidence`
- `ordered_position`
- `list_kind`
- `manual_review_required` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

This is the **interpretation layer**.

### 3. `projected_target`
How that interpretation will be represented in the DOCX.

Examples:
- `projected_slide_heading_text`
- `projected_slide_heading_style`
- `projection_status` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

This is the **output-mapping layer**.

That separation is the most important design choice in the schema.

## Why that separation matters

A PowerPoint slide is a mess, semantically speaking.

A title-looking thing might be:

- a real title placeholder
- the first line of a body placeholder
- a random text box
- grouped content
- a table cell
- text embedded in something visually meaningful but structurally ambiguous

The schema does **not** pretend that appearance alone is enough. Instead it records:

- what it saw
- what it inferred
- how confident it is
- whether a human needs to review it

That is why the manifests contain flags like:

- `NO_TITLE_PLACEHOLDER_FOUND`
- `STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN`
- `TABLE_CELL_TEXT_EXTRACTED`
- `GROUPED_CONTENT_DETECTED`
- `CONNECTOR_RELATIONSHIP_WARNING`
- `DESCRIPTION_METADATA_MISSING`
- `NO_EXTRACTABLE_TEXT` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

So the schemaŌĆÖs logic is: **preserve ambiguity instead of hiding it**.

## Why `manual_review_required` is everywhere

Because the pipeline is not claiming full semantic certainty.

You can see `manual_review_required` at multiple levels:

- slide
- text block
- visual entry
- warning entry

That means the manifest is not just data. It is also a **review queue**.

The converter is effectively saying:

- ŌĆ£This part is safe enough to project automatically.ŌĆØ
- ŌĆ£This part is ambiguous and should survive into review.ŌĆØ
- ŌĆ£This part probably loses meaning unless someone checks it.ŌĆØ

That is a strong design choice, not an accident. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

## Why the text model is shaped the way it is

Inside each `text_block_entry`, you get:

- source container type
- placeholder type
- shape name
- ordered position
- interpreted role
- role confidence
- paragraph entries

And each paragraph keeps:

- `text`
- `level`
- `list_kind`
- `list_marker_hint`

That tells you the target is not ŌĆ£preserve slide layout.ŌĆØ The target is ŌĆ£recover enough structure to rebuild readable Word content.ŌĆØ

So:

- `level` helps reconstruct nesting/indentation
- `list_kind` helps reconstruct plain text vs bullets vs numbered lists
- `ordered_position` helps reconstruct a reading order
- `auto_merge_allowed: false` is a guardrail against careless flattening ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü

This is much closer to **content recovery for accessible authoring** than to visual conversion.

## Why tables are flattened into text blocks with warnings

This is one of the clearest signs of the schemaŌĆÖs philosophy.

When the source is a PowerPoint table cell, the manifest does **not** silently claim ŌĆ£great, we have a semantic table.ŌĆØ Instead it records the cell as a text block and raises `TABLE_CELL_TEXT_EXTRACTED`. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

That tells you the pipeline knows:

- PowerPoint table extraction is not the same thing as having a valid Word table structure
- table semantics may need reconstruction
- extracted text alone is not enough to assume accessibility

So the logic is conservative:
**extract first, warn second, trust later only if reviewed.**

## Why visuals are modeled separately

Each `visual_entry` keeps:

- a label
- a visual type
- a description
- a description source
- whether manual review is required

This is because images are a separate accessibility problem from text extraction.

The manifests are explicitly tracking whether visual description metadata exists or is missing. That is why files with lots of missing descriptions show high counts in `detected_visual_entries_missing_descriptions` and lots of `DESCRIPTION_METADATA_MISSING` warnings. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü

So the logic is:

- text recovery and visual-description recovery are separate channels
- both need to survive into the DOCX workflow
- missing visual meaning must remain visible as a problem, not disappear

## Why DOCX is the target instead of ŌĆ£fixing the PPTXŌĆØ

Because DOCX is being used as the **reviewable semantic companion**.

The schema is geared toward producing a Word document where reviewers can:

- see one heading per slide
- move through slides in a linear reading order
- inspect extracted text
- inspect extracted visuals
- identify ambiguities
- repair structure more easily than in raw PPTX layout space

That is why every slide becomes something like:

- `Slide 7. Purpose and Organization of the DSM-5-TR`
- style `Heading1`
- separator `PAGE_BREAK` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

The target is not slide fidelity. The target is **linearized, editable, accessibility-friendly review space**.

## The shortest accurate description

The schemaŌĆÖs logic is:

> **Observe the PPTX honestly, normalize cautiously, project into DOCX deterministically, and preserve every ambiguity as an explicit review artifact.**

More concretely:

- `observed_source` = what was literally found
- `normalized_workflow` = best-effort interpretation
- `projected_target` = how it will be represented in Word
- `warning_entries` + `manual_review_required` = places where the converter refuses to fake certainty

That is why the schema feels so repetitive. It is not redundant by accident. It is designed to keep **source evidence, interpretation, target mapping, and uncertainty** separate.

If you want, I can turn that into a one-page **field-by-field logic map** for the whole manifest.
swipes:
## The logic behind it

This schema is not trying to be a full semantic model of the deck. It is a **workflow manifest for a controlled conversion pipeline**:

**PPTX source ŌåÆ extracted/interpreted slide content ŌåÆ review flags ŌåÆ projected DOCX companion**

You can see that logic directly in the top-level structure:

- `source_package` describes the input as an OOXML/OPC/PresentationML package
- `target_package` describes the output as an OOXML/OPC/WordprocessingML package
- `run_config` records conversion choices like preview images and `PAGE_BREAK`
- `slide_entries` is the actual per-slide conversion record ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

So the manifest is basically a **receipt plus decision log** for how a slide deck is being turned into a Word companion document.

## Why PPTX to DOCX specifically

The design assumption is:

- **PPTX is the visual source**
- **DOCX is the editable accessibility-review target**

That is why every slide has a `projected_target` object with fields like:

- `projected_slide_heading_text`
- `projected_slide_heading_style`
- `projected_slide_separator_mode`
- `projection_status`

The pipeline is saying: ŌĆ£For this slide, here is what will become the corresponding Word heading and where the break goes.ŌĆØ In other words, each slide is being turned into a **Word section/chunk** rather than kept as a slide-native visual object. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

## The core structural idea

Each slide entry is split into **three layers**:

### 1. `observed_source`
What the converter literally found in the PPTX.

Examples:
- which slide XML part it came from
- whether a text block was a `placeholder_text`, `standalone_text_box`, or `table_cell`
- the original PowerPoint shape name
- preview image candidates ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

This is the **raw evidence layer**.

### 2. `normalized_workflow`
What the converter thinks that source element probably *means* for the workflow.

Examples:
- `chosen_title`
- `title_source`
- `interpreted_text_role`
- `role_confidence`
- `ordered_position`
- `list_kind`
- `manual_review_required` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

This is the **interpretation layer**.

### 3. `projected_target`
How that interpretation will be represented in the DOCX.

Examples:
- `projected_slide_heading_text`
- `projected_slide_heading_style`
- `projection_status` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

This is the **output-mapping layer**.

That separation is the most important design choice in the schema.

## Why that separation matters

A PowerPoint slide is a mess, semantically speaking.

A title-looking thing might be:

- a real title placeholder
- the first line of a body placeholder
- a random text box
- grouped content
- a table cell
- text embedded in something visually meaningful but structurally ambiguous

The schema does **not** pretend that appearance alone is enough. Instead it records:

- what it saw
- what it inferred
- how confident it is
- whether a human needs to review it

That is why the manifests contain flags like:

- `NO_TITLE_PLACEHOLDER_FOUND`
- `STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN`
- `TABLE_CELL_TEXT_EXTRACTED`
- `GROUPED_CONTENT_DETECTED`
- `CONNECTOR_RELATIONSHIP_WARNING`
- `DESCRIPTION_METADATA_MISSING`
- `NO_EXTRACTABLE_TEXT` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

So the schemaŌĆÖs logic is: **preserve ambiguity instead of hiding it**.

## Why `manual_review_required` is everywhere

Because the pipeline is not claiming full semantic certainty.

You can see `manual_review_required` at multiple levels:

- slide
- text block
- visual entry
- warning entry

That means the manifest is not just data. It is also a **review queue**.

The converter is effectively saying:

- ŌĆ£This part is safe enough to project automatically.ŌĆØ
- ŌĆ£This part is ambiguous and should survive into review.ŌĆØ
- ŌĆ£This part probably loses meaning unless someone checks it.ŌĆØ

That is a strong design choice, not an accident. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

## Why the text model is shaped the way it is

Inside each `text_block_entry`, you get:

- source container type
- placeholder type
- shape name
- ordered position
- interpreted role
- role confidence
- paragraph entries

And each paragraph keeps:

- `text`
- `level`
- `list_kind`
- `list_marker_hint`

That tells you the target is not ŌĆ£preserve slide layout.ŌĆØ The target is ŌĆ£recover enough structure to rebuild readable Word content.ŌĆØ

So:

- `level` helps reconstruct nesting/indentation
- `list_kind` helps reconstruct plain text vs bullets vs numbered lists
- `ordered_position` helps reconstruct a reading order
- `auto_merge_allowed: false` is a guardrail against careless flattening ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü

This is much closer to **content recovery for accessible authoring** than to visual conversion.

## Why tables are flattened into text blocks with warnings

This is one of the clearest signs of the schemaŌĆÖs philosophy.

When the source is a PowerPoint table cell, the manifest does **not** silently claim ŌĆ£great, we have a semantic table.ŌĆØ Instead it records the cell as a text block and raises `TABLE_CELL_TEXT_EXTRACTED`. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

That tells you the pipeline knows:

- PowerPoint table extraction is not the same thing as having a valid Word table structure
- table semantics may need reconstruction
- extracted text alone is not enough to assume accessibility

So the logic is conservative:
**extract first, warn second, trust later only if reviewed.**

## Why visuals are modeled separately

Each `visual_entry` keeps:

- a label
- a visual type
- a description
- a description source
- whether manual review is required

This is because images are a separate accessibility problem from text extraction.

The manifests are explicitly tracking whether visual description metadata exists or is missing. That is why files with lots of missing descriptions show high counts in `detected_visual_entries_missing_descriptions` and lots of `DESCRIPTION_METADATA_MISSING` warnings. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü

So the logic is:

- text recovery and visual-description recovery are separate channels
- both need to survive into the DOCX workflow
- missing visual meaning must remain visible as a problem, not disappear

## Why DOCX is the target instead of ŌĆ£fixing the PPTXŌĆØ

Because DOCX is being used as the **reviewable semantic companion**.

The schema is geared toward producing a Word document where reviewers can:

- see one heading per slide
- move through slides in a linear reading order
- inspect extracted text
- inspect extracted visuals
- identify ambiguities
- repair structure more easily than in raw PPTX layout space

That is why every slide becomes something like:

- `Slide 7. Purpose and Organization of the DSM-5-TR`
- style `Heading1`
- separator `PAGE_BREAK` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

The target is not slide fidelity. The target is **linearized, editable, accessibility-friendly review space**.

## The shortest accurate description

The schemaŌĆÖs logic is:

> **Observe the PPTX honestly, normalize cautiously, project into DOCX deterministically, and preserve every ambiguity as an explicit review artifact.**

More concretely:

- `observed_source` = what was literally found
- `normalized_workflow` = best-effort interpretation
- `projected_target` = how it will be represented in Word
- `warning_entries` + `manual_review_required` = places where the converter refuses to fake certainty

That is why the schema feels so repetitive. It is not redundant by accident. It is designed to keep **source evidence, interpretation, target mapping, and uncertainty** separate.

If you want, I can turn that into a one-page **field-by-field logic map** for the whole manifest.

mes:
Yes.

That is the core pipeline logic:

## The workflow

1. **Input**
   - PPTX package metadata and source slide parts
   - `source_package`
   - slide `observed_source` like `package_part`, `presentationml_root`, preview image candidates ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

2. **Extract**
   - pull out text containers, paragraphs, visuals, and source shape info
   - `text_block_entries`
   - `paragraph_entries`
   - `visual_entries` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü

3. **Interpret / normalize**
   - choose a title
   - classify text role
   - assign ordered position
   - classify list behavior
   - record confidence
   - `normalized_workflow` at slide, text-block, paragraph, and visual levels ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

4. **Review flags**
   - preserve uncertainty instead of hiding it
   - `warning_entries`
   - `manual_review_required`
   - examples: missing descriptions, uncertain text-box role, grouped content, connector meaning, no extractable text, numbered-sequence review ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü

5. **Output / projection**
   - map the normalized result into a DOCX companion structure
   - `projected_target`
   - slide heading text, heading style, separator mode, projection status ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

## Even shorter

Yes:

**input ŌåÆ extract ŌåÆ interpret/normalize ŌåÆ flag uncertainty ŌåÆ project to DOCX**

The only tweak IŌĆÖd make is that ŌĆ£review flagsŌĆØ are not just a side step. They are a first-class output of the pipeline, alongside the projected DOCX mapping.

So the most precise version is:

**input ŌåÆ extract ŌåÆ interpret/normalize ŌåÆ attach review flags ŌåÆ project target output**

If you want, IŌĆÖll turn that into a compact architecture diagram in plain text.

swipes: IŌĆÖm translating the existing PPTXŌåÆDOCX manifest logic into a PDFŌåÆDOCX version. The big design change is that PDF is less trustworthy as a semantic source, so the schema needs stronger distinction between ŌĆ£found in PDF structureŌĆØ and ŌĆ£inferred from layout/text.ŌĆØ

name: Assistant
mes:
Yes. That pipeline makes sense.

## The short answer

You can absolutely re-envision it as:

**input PDF ŌåÆ extract ŌåÆ interpret/normalize ŌåÆ attach review flags ŌåÆ output DOCX**

But the logic changes in an important way:

- **PPTX** gives you slide-native containers like placeholders, text boxes, tables, and shape descriptions.
- **PDF** gives you a mix of text layer, page geometry, annotations, metadata, maybe tags, maybe outline, maybe forms, and often a lot less trustworthy semantics.

So the PDF version needs to be **more explicit about provenance and confidence**.

## What stays the same

The core workflow stays intact:

1. **Input**
2. **Extract**
3. **Interpret / normalize**
4. **Attach review flags**
5. **Project to DOCX**

That is already the logic of the current manifests, just with PPTX as source and slides as the unit of organization. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü

## What changes with PDF as input

### 1. The source evidence layer changes

For PPTX, `observed_source` is things like:

- `package_part`
- `source_text_container_type`
- `placeholder_type`
- `shape_name` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

For PDF, the equivalent evidence layer would need different fields, such as:

- `page_number`
- `object_ref` or annotation/form reference if available
- `text_source_type` such as `tagged_text`, `untagged_text`, `ocr_text`, `annotation_text`, `form_field_text`
- `bbox`
- `font_name`
- `font_size`
- `reading_order_source`
- `structure_source`
- `outline_source`
- `metadata_source`

So instead of ŌĆ£this came from a PowerPoint title placeholder,ŌĆØ youŌĆÖd say things like:

- ŌĆ£this came from a tagged `H1` structure elementŌĆØ
- ŌĆ£this came from untagged positioned text on page 4ŌĆØ
- ŌĆ£this came from OCR text over an image pageŌĆØ
- ŌĆ£this came from a link annotationŌĆØ
- ŌĆ£this came from the document outline/bookmarksŌĆØ

## 2. The organizing unit may stop being ŌĆ£pageŌĆØ

With PPTX, a **slide** is a natural unit.

With PDF, a **page** is not always the best semantic unit.

A better model is probably:

- **document-level**
- **section/block-level**
- **page-level evidence**

Because a PDFŌĆÖs real structure may cross page boundaries. A heading on page 3 may govern content on pages 3ŌĆō7. A table may continue across pages. A list may break across pages.

So I would not just rename `slide_entries` to `page_entries` and call it done.

I would probably use something more like:

- `document_entry`
- `content_block_entries`
- optional `page_entries` for source evidence / layout anchoring

## 3. PDF needs stronger provenance tracking

This is the biggest change.

In PPTX, if text comes from a title placeholder, that is already a strong clue.

In PDF, the same extracted paragraph might come from:

- a real structure tree heading
- visually large untagged text
- a repeated running header
- OCR over an image
- a caption next to a figure
- a decorative text artifact that should not be semantic at all

So the normalized layer needs stronger provenance, like:

```json id="pdf-normalized-provenance-example"
{
  "interpreted_role": "heading_candidate",
  "role_confidence": "medium",
  "role_basis": [
    "tag_tree:H1",
    "font_size_outlier",
    "top_of_page_position"
  ],
  "semantic_source_quality": "high"
}
```

Or:

```json id="pdf-normalized-provenance-example-2"
{
  "interpreted_role": "body_content",
  "role_confidence": "low",
  "role_basis": [
    "ocr_text_block",
    "layout_cluster_only"
  ],
  "semantic_source_quality": "low"
}
```

That kind of distinction matters much more for PDF than for PPTX.

## 4. Review flags become more important

The current manifests already use warning objects and `manual_review_required` heavily. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

For PDF, youŌĆÖd want many more review-flag types, for example:

- `NO_TAG_TREE_PRESENT`
- `TAG_TREE_PRESENT_BUT_INCOMPLETE`
- `READING_ORDER_AMBIGUOUS`
- `OCR_TEXT_DETECTED`
- `OCR_CONFIDENCE_LOW`
- `PAGE_IMAGE_ONLY`
- `RUNNING_HEADER_OR_FOOTER_SUSPECTED`
- `HEADING_INFERRED_FROM_LAYOUT`
- `TABLE_DETECTED_BUT_HEADER_RELATIONS_UNCERTAIN`
- `CAPTION_FIGURE_ASSOCIATION_UNCERTAIN`
- `LINK_TEXT_PRESENT_BUT_DESTINATION_UNCLEAR`
- `FORM_FIELD_LABEL_UNCERTAIN`
- `DECORATIVE_VS_INFORMATIVE_FIGURE_UNCERTAIN`

That would be the PDF analogue of the current PPTX warnings like `STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN`, `TABLE_CELL_TEXT_EXTRACTED`, and `DESCRIPTION_METADATA_MISSING`. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

## 5. The output DOCX should be section-oriented, not page-oriented

For PPTX, outputting one Heading 1 per slide makes sense. That is exactly what the current manifests project. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

For PDF, the DOCX output should usually be built around **normalized document structure**, not one heading per page.

So the DOCX projection might become:

- document title
- section headings
- subsections
- paragraphs
- lists
- tables
- figures with descriptions/captions
- appendix of unresolved review flags
- optional page-break markers for traceability

That way the DOCX becomes a **reviewable semantic companion**, not just a page dump.

## Two modes would make sense

### Mode 1: Structure-aware PDF
For PDFs with tags, outline, forms, metadata, maybe decent semantics.

Pipeline emphasis:
- trust structure first
- use layout as secondary evidence
- fewer review flags

### Mode 2: Layout/OCR recovery PDF
For untagged, image-heavy, or badly structured PDFs.

Pipeline emphasis:
- extract text, geometry, OCR, annotations
- infer structure cautiously
- attach many review flags
- never hide uncertainty

That split is important because the same schema should be able to express both.

## What the schema might look like

A rough top-level shape could be:

```json id="pdf-to-docx-manifest-top-level"
{
  "manifest_version": "1.0",
  "manifest_kind": "pdf_to_docx_accessibility_manifest",
  "source_package": {
    "format_family": "PDF",
    "input_file_name": "string",
    "input_file_path": "string"
  },
  "target_package": {
    "format_family": "OOXML",
    "package_model": "OPC",
    "document_vocabulary": "WordprocessingML",
    "draft_output_file_name": "string",
    "draft_output_file_path": "string"
  },
  "run_config": { ... },
  "document_summary": { ... },
  "document_warning_entries": [ ... ],
  "document_structure_entries": [ ... ],
  "page_entries": [ ... ]
}
```

And then each normalized content block might have:

```json id="pdf-content-block-schema-sketch"
{
  "block_id": "string",
  "page_span": [1, 2],
  "observed_sources": [ ... ],
  "normalized_workflow": {
    "interpreted_role": "heading|paragraph|list|table|figure|caption|link|form_field|artifact|unknown",
    "role_confidence": "high|medium|low",
    "role_basis": [ ... ],
    "manual_review_required": true
  },
  "warning_entries": [ ... ],
  "projected_target": { ... }
}
```

## The logic in one sentence

For PPTX, the manifest says:

> ŌĆ£Here is what we found in slide-native authoring structure.ŌĆØ

For PDF, it would need to say:

> ŌĆ£Here is what we found in the PDF, here is how much of that was explicit structure versus inference, and here is how we are projecting it into editable Word structure.ŌĆØ

## My blunt recommendation

Yes, re-envisioning it as **PDF ŌåÆ DOCX** makes sense.

But I would **not** do a shallow rename of the current PPTX manifest.

I would redesign it around:

- **document/section-level normalized structure**
- **page-level evidence**
- **strong provenance tracking**
- **explicit distinction between extracted semantics and inferred semantics**
- **review flags as first-class outputs**

That is the version that actually fits PDF.

If you want, IŌĆÖll draft a concrete **v0.1 PDFŌåÆDOCX manifest schema** next.

In PPTX, if text comes from a title placeholder, that is already a strong clue.

In PDF, the same extracted paragraph might come from:

- a real structure tree heading
- visually large untagged text
- a repeated running header
- OCR over an image
- a caption next to a figure
- a decorative text artifact that should not be semantic at all

So the normalized layer needs stronger provenance, like:

```json id="pdf-normalized-provenance-example"
{
  "interpreted_role": "heading_candidate",
  "role_confidence": "medium",
  "role_basis": [
    "tag_tree:H1",
    "font_size_outlier",
    "top_of_page_position"
  ],
  "semantic_source_quality": "high"
}
```

Or:

```json id="pdf-normalized-provenance-example-2"
{
  "interpreted_role": "body_content",
  "role_confidence": "low",
  "role_basis": [
    "ocr_text_block",
    "layout_cluster_only"
  ],
  "semantic_source_quality": "low"
}
```

That kind of distinction matters much more for PDF than for PPTX.

## 4. Review flags become more important

The current manifests already use warning objects and `manual_review_required` heavily. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file1Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file2Ņłü

For PDF, youŌĆÖd want many more review-flag types, for example:

- `NO_TAG_TREE_PRESENT`
- `TAG_TREE_PRESENT_BUT_INCOMPLETE`
- `READING_ORDER_AMBIGUOUS`
- `OCR_TEXT_DETECTED`
- `OCR_CONFIDENCE_LOW`
- `PAGE_IMAGE_ONLY`
- `RUNNING_HEADER_OR_FOOTER_SUSPECTED`
- `HEADING_INFERRED_FROM_LAYOUT`
- `TABLE_DETECTED_BUT_HEADER_RELATIONS_UNCERTAIN`
- `CAPTION_FIGURE_ASSOCIATION_UNCERTAIN`
- `LINK_TEXT_PRESENT_BUT_DESTINATION_UNCLEAR`
- `FORM_FIELD_LABEL_UNCERTAIN`
- `DECORATIVE_VS_INFORMATIVE_FIGURE_UNCERTAIN`

That would be the PDF analogue of the current PPTX warnings like `STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN`, `TABLE_CELL_TEXT_EXTRACTED`, and `DESCRIPTION_METADATA_MISSING`. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

## 5. The output DOCX should be section-oriented, not page-oriented

For PPTX, outputting one Heading 1 per slide makes sense. That is exactly what the current manifests project. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn11file0Ņłü

For PDF, the DOCX output should usually be built around **normalized document structure**, not one heading per page.

So the DOCX projection might become:

- document title
- section headings
- subsections
- paragraphs
- lists
- tables
- figures with descriptions/captions
- appendix of unresolved review flags
- optional page-break markers for traceability

That way the DOCX becomes a **reviewable semantic companion**, not just a page dump.

## Two modes would make sense

### Mode 1: Structure-aware PDF
For PDFs with tags, outline, forms, metadata, maybe decent semantics.

Pipeline emphasis:
- trust structure first
- use layout as secondary evidence
- fewer review flags

### Mode 2: Layout/OCR recovery PDF
For untagged, image-heavy, or badly structured PDFs.

Pipeline emphasis:
- extract text, geometry, OCR, annotations
- infer structure cautiously
- attach many review flags
- never hide uncertainty

That split is important because the same schema should be able to express both.

## What the schema might look like

A rough top-level shape could be:

```json id="pdf-to-docx-manifest-top-level"
{
  "manifest_version": "1.0",
  "manifest_kind": "pdf_to_docx_accessibility_manifest",
  "source_package": {
    "format_family": "PDF",
    "input_file_name": "string",
    "input_file_path": "string"
  },
  "target_package": {
    "format_family": "OOXML",
    "package_model": "OPC",
    "document_vocabulary": "WordprocessingML",
    "draft_output_file_name": "string",
    "draft_output_file_path": "string"
  },
  "run_config": { ... },
  "document_summary": { ... },
  "document_warning_entries": [ ... ],
  "document_structure_entries": [ ... ],
  "page_entries": [ ... ]
}
```

And then each normalized content block might have:

```json id="pdf-content-block-schema-sketch"
{
  "block_id": "string",
  "page_span": [1, 2],
  "observed_sources": [ ... ],
  "normalized_workflow": {
    "interpreted_role": "heading|paragraph|list|table|figure|caption|link|form_field|artifact|unknown",
    "role_confidence": "high|medium|low",
    "role_basis": [ ... ],
    "manual_review_required": true
  },
  "warning_entries": [ ... ],
  "projected_target": { ... }
}
```

## The logic in one sentence

For PPTX, the manifest says:

> ŌĆ£Here is what we found in slide-native authoring structure.ŌĆØ

For PDF, it would need to say:

> ŌĆ£Here is what we found in the PDF, here is how much of that was explicit structure versus inference, and here is how we are projecting it into editable Word structure.ŌĆØ

## My blunt recommendation

Yes, re-envisioning it as **PDF ŌåÆ DOCX** makes sense.

But I would **not** do a shallow rename of the current PPTX manifest.

I would redesign it around:

- **document/section-level normalized structure**
- **page-level evidence**
- **strong provenance tracking**
- **explicit distinction between extracted semantics and inferred semantics**
- **review flags as first-class outputs**

That is the version that actually fits PDF.

If you want, IŌĆÖll draft a concrete **v0.1 PDFŌåÆDOCX manifest schema** next.

name: Assistant
mes:
Yes. This is worth doing.

The PPTX manifests already gave us the right **workflow spine**:

- `observed_source`
- `normalized_workflow`
- `warning_entries`
- `projected_target` ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn12file11Ņłü

And the markdown spec gave us the right **destination rigor**: explicit reading order, explicit node types, explicit artifact handling, explicit tag mapping, and rejection instead of guessing. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn12file2Ņłü ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn12file3Ņłü

## The real move

We should **not** start by trying to make PDF behave like PPTX.

We should start by building a **PDF evidence manifest** that feeds a DOCX companion workflow.

So the pipeline becomes:

- input PDF
- extract evidence
- normalize into candidate structure
- attach review flags
- project to DOCX

## What changes from the PPTX version

PPTX extraction gets clues from placeholders, text boxes, slide layout, and table cells.

PDF extraction needs stronger provenance fields, because the same text might come from:

- a real tag tree
- the outline/bookmarks
- positioned untagged text
- OCR text
- a form field
- an annotation
- a repeated header/footer
- a caption near a figure

So the PDF version has to care much more about:

- **where the evidence came from**
- **how trustworthy that source is**
- **whether the role was extracted or inferred**

## The design principle

For PDF, every normalized block should answer three separate questions:

1. **What did we literally find?**
2. **What do we think it means?**
3. **Why do we think that, and how confident are we?**

That is the piece that will keep the project honest.

## I think the right schema shape is

### Top level

```json id="pdf-docx-manifest-v0-top-level"
{
  "manifest_version": "0.1",
  "manifest_kind": "pdf_to_docx_accessibility_manifest",
  "source_package": {},
  "target_package": {},
  "run_config": {},
  "document_summary": {},
  "document_warning_entries": [],
  "document_structure_entries": [],
  "page_entries": []
}
```

### Source package

```json id="pdf-docx-source-package-v0"
{
  "format_family": "PDF",
  "input_file_name": "string",
  "input_file_path": "string",
  "page_count": 0,
  "tagged_pdf_detected": false,
  "outline_detected": false,
  "forms_detected": false,
  "annotations_detected": false,
  "ocr_text_detected": false
}
```

### Page evidence

```json id="pdf-docx-page-entry-v0"
{
  "page_number": 1,
  "observed_source": {
    "page_bbox": [0, 0, 612, 792],
    "text_layer_detected": true,
    "ocr_text_detected": false,
    "struct_tree_detected": true,
    "annotation_count": 0,
    "form_field_count": 0,
    "image_count": 0
  },
  "raw_block_entries": [],
  "warning_entries": []
}
```

### Raw extracted block

```json id="pdf-docx-raw-block-entry-v0"
{
  "block_id": "p1_b12",
  "source_evidence_type": "tagged_text",
  "source_ref": "page=1;mcid=12",
  "bbox": [72, 96, 468, 118],
  "text": "Introduction",
  "style_hints": {
    "font_name": "ABCDEE+Helvetica-Bold",
    "font_size": 24
  },
  "structure_hints": {
    "tag_name": "H1",
    "parent_tag_name": "Sect"
  }
}
```

### Normalized structure block

```json id="pdf-docx-normalized-block-entry-v0"
{
  "block_id": "n0001",
  "page_span": [1, 1],
  "observed_source_refs": ["p1_b12"],
  "normalized_workflow": {
    "interpreted_role": "heading",
    "heading_level": 1,
    "role_confidence": "high",
    "role_basis": [
      "tag_tree:H1"
    ],
    "reading_order_index": 1,
    "manual_review_required": false
  },
  "warning_entries": [],
  "projected_target": {
    "target_package_part": "word/document.xml",
    "target_style": "Heading1",
    "projection_status": "planned"
  }
}
```

## The critical provenance field

This is the one I do not want us to skip:

```json id="pdf-docx-role-basis-example"
{
  "role_basis": [
    "tag_tree:H1",
    "outline_match",
    "font_size_outlier",
    "top_of_page_position"
  ]
}
```

That lets us distinguish:

- **extracted semantics**
- **layout inference**
- **OCR recovery**
- **outline-derived structure**
- **annotation-derived structure**

Without that, the PDF version will get mushy fast.

## Review flags weŌĆÖll need immediately

A good first set:

- `NO_TEXT_LAYER`
- `OCR_TEXT_DETECTED`
- `NO_TAG_TREE_PRESENT`
- `TAG_TREE_PARTIAL`
- `HEADING_INFERRED_FROM_LAYOUT`
- `READING_ORDER_AMBIGUOUS`
- `REPEATED_HEADER_OR_FOOTER_SUSPECTED`
- `TABLE_RELATIONSHIPS_UNCERTAIN`
- `CAPTION_FIGURE_ASSOCIATION_UNCERTAIN`
- `FORM_FIELD_LABEL_UNCERTAIN`
- `ANNOTATION_FUNCTION_UNCERTAIN`
- `DECORATIVE_VS_INFORMATIVE_FIGURE_UNCERTAIN`

That gives us the PDF version of what the PPTX manifests are already doing with things like `STANDALONE_TEXT_BOX_ROLE_UNCERTAIN`, `TABLE_CELL_TEXT_EXTRACTED`, and `DESCRIPTION_METADATA_MISSING`. ŅłĆfileciteŅłéturn12file5Ņłü

## The right build order

We should do this in this order:

1. **Lock the top-level manifest spine**
   - source package
   - document summary
   - page entries
   - normalized structure entries

2. **Define raw PDF evidence types**
   - tagged text
   - untagged positioned text
   - OCR text
   - annotation text
   - form field text
   - outline/bookmark entry
   - image/figure evidence

3. **Define normalized roles**
   - heading
   - paragraph
   - list
   - list item
   - table
   - figure
   - caption
   - link
   - note
   - form field
   - artifact
   - unknown

4. **Define warning taxonomy**
   - extraction problems
   - semantic uncertainty
   - accessibility-risk flags

5. **Define DOCX projection rules**
   - heading mapping
   - paragraph mapping
   - list mapping
   - table mapping
   - figure/caption mapping
   - appendix/report mapping for unresolved issues

## My blunt read

Yes, IŌĆÖm up for it.

And I think this is actually a better problem than the PPTX one, because the difficulty forces us to separate:

- evidence
- interpretation
- confidence
- output

more rigorously.

The next right step is to draft **PDFŌåÆDOCX manifest v0.1** as a literal field contract.

name: Assistant
mes:
Yes ŌĆö **for a v0.1 PDF ŌåÆ DOCX evidence manifest, PDF.js can extract a lot of what we need**.

## What it can cover well

At the **document level**, PDF.js can give us:

- raw PDF bytes with `getData()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn612831view1Ņłü
- metadata with `getMetadata()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view1Ņłü
- outline/bookmarks with `getOutline()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view2Ņłü
- permissions with `getPermissions()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view3Ņłü
- form field objects with `getFieldObjects()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view4Ņłü
- MarkInfo flags with `getMarkInfo()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view0Ņłü

At the **page level**, PDF.js can give us:

- extracted text with `getTextContent()` or `streamTextContent()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view5Ņłü
- annotations with `getAnnotations()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view7Ņłü
- the page structure tree with `getStructTree()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view6Ņłü
- page drawing/operator info with `getOperatorList()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view8Ņłü
- XFA page content with `getXfa()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view9Ņłü

So for a first manifest, PDF.js is enough to populate things like:

- document metadata
- page count
- whether MarkInfo exists
- whether outline/bookmarks exist
- whether form fields exist
- page-by-page text extraction
- page-by-page structure tree presence/content
- page annotations
- XFA presence
- permissions and some interactive features ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view0Ņłéturn801183view1Ņłéturn801183view2Ņłéturn801183view3Ņłéturn801183view4Ņłéturn801183view5Ņłéturn801183view6Ņłéturn801183view7Ņłéturn801183view9Ņłü

## Where it falls short

This is the important part.

### 1. OCR
PDF.js is **not** the OCR step. PDF.js describes its public display layer as exposing an easier API to render PDFs and get information out of them, but OCR is not part of that surface. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn612831view0Ņłü

So if the input PDF is image-only or partly image-only, we need another tool for OCR.

### 2. Whole-document structure tree in one clean public call
The public API gives us `getStructTree()` on **`PDFPageProxy`**, which means **page-by-page** structure extraction. I did not find a documented one-call document-level structure-tree method on `PDFDocumentProxy`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view6Ņłéturn612831view1Ņłü

So yes, we can get structure ŌĆö but we may need to assemble the document view ourselves from page-level results.

### 3. Stable low-level internals
PDF.js explicitly says the **core layer** is advanced and likely to change, and that the documented supported API is the **display layer**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn612831view0Ņłü

So if later we need deeper object-graph traversal, that may exist, but it is outside the stable supported surface.

### 4. Perfect text fidelity
`getTextContent()` normalizes whitespace: the docs say all whitespace is replaced by standard spaces. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view5Ņłü

That is fine for a review manifest, but it matters if you were hoping for byte-faithful text reconstruction.

## My actual recommendation

For **v0.1**, yes:

- use **PDF.js as the primary extraction engine**
- design the schema around **page-level evidence plus document-level summary**
- assume **OCR is external**
- assume **whole-document semantic structure may need to be reconstructed from per-page structure results**
- keep strong provenance/confidence fields in the manifest

## The blunt version

**PDF.js can extract enough for us to start seriously.**  
Not enough to make the whole problem magically easy, but enough to build the first real PDF ŌåÆ DOCX manifest pipeline without pretending.

If you want, IŌĆÖll draft the **v0.1 field contract** next around exactly what PDF.js can already supply.

- whether outline/bookmarks exist
- whether form fields exist
- page-by-page text extraction
- page-by-page structure tree presence/content
- page annotations
- XFA presence
- permissions and some interactive features ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view0Ņłéturn801183view1Ņłéturn801183view2Ņłéturn801183view3Ņłéturn801183view4Ņłéturn801183view5Ņłéturn801183view6Ņłéturn801183view7Ņłéturn801183view9Ņłü

## Where it falls short

This is the important part.

### 1. OCR
PDF.js is **not** the OCR step. PDF.js describes its public display layer as exposing an easier API to render PDFs and get information out of them, but OCR is not part of that surface. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn612831view0Ņłü

So if the input PDF is image-only or partly image-only, we need another tool for OCR.

### 2. Whole-document structure tree in one clean public call
The public API gives us `getStructTree()` on **`PDFPageProxy`**, which means **page-by-page** structure extraction. I did not find a documented one-call document-level structure-tree method on `PDFDocumentProxy`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view6Ņłéturn612831view1Ņłü

So yes, we can get structure ŌĆö but we may need to assemble the document view ourselves from page-level results.

### 3. Stable low-level internals
PDF.js explicitly says the **core layer** is advanced and likely to change, and that the documented supported API is the **display layer**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn612831view0Ņłü

So if later we need deeper object-graph traversal, that may exist, but it is outside the stable supported surface.

### 4. Perfect text fidelity
`getTextContent()` normalizes whitespace: the docs say all whitespace is replaced by standard spaces. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn801183view5Ņłü

That is fine for a review manifest, but it matters if you were hoping for byte-faithful text reconstruction.

## My actual recommendation

For **v0.1**, yes:

- use **PDF.js as the primary extraction engine**
- design the schema around **page-level evidence plus document-level summary**
- assume **OCR is external**
- assume **whole-document semantic structure may need to be reconstructed from per-page structure results**
- keep strong provenance/confidence fields in the manifest

## The blunt version

**PDF.js can extract enough for us to start seriously.**  
Not enough to make the whole problem magically easy, but enough to build the first real PDF ŌåÆ DOCX manifest pipeline without pretending.

If you want, IŌĆÖll draft the **v0.1 field contract** next around exactly what PDF.js can already supply.

Yes. Quite a bit more.

So far we were mostly talking about the accessibility-facing subset. PDF.js can also pull a bunch of **document mechanics**, **navigation data**, **interactive data**, and **page-level technical data** that could be useful even if they never end up in the DOCX schema. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn838487view0Ņłéturn838487view1Ņłéturn358629view2Ņłéturn358629view3Ņłéturn358629view4Ņłü

## Probably useful for the manifest

### Document identity and package facts
- `numPages`
- `fingerprints` for document identity
- `getData()` for raw PDF bytes
- `getDownloadInfo()` for file size in bytes ŅłĆciteŅłéturn838487view0Ņłéturn358629view3Ņłü

### Navigation and structure
- `getOutline()` for bookmark tree
- `getDestination(id)` and `getDestinations()` for named destinations
- `getPageLabels()` for printed/logical page labels
- `getOpenAction()` for document open destination/action
- `getPageLayout()`
- `getPageMode()`
- `getViewerPreferences()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn358629view2Ņłéturn358629view3Ņłéturn358629view4Ņłü

### Layers and optional content
- `getOptionalContentConfig()` to inspect optional content groups/layers in PDFs that use them ŅłĆciteŅłéturn358629view2Ņłü

### Attachments and embedded content
- `getAttachments()` for embedded files/attachments ŅłĆciteŅłéturn358629view3Ņłü

### Forms and scripting
- `getFieldObjects()`
- `getCalculationOrderIds()`
- `getJSActions()`
- `hasJSActions()`
- `annotationStorage`
- `isPureXfa`
- `allXfaHtml`
- page-level `getXfa()` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn838487view0Ņłéturn358629view3Ņłéturn358629view4Ņłéturn838487view1Ņłü

### Annotation-level extraction
- page-level `getAnnotations()`
- document-level `getAnnotationsByType()` to pull selected annotation types across the document ŅłĆciteŅłéturn358629view0Ņłéturn358629view3Ņłü

### Page geometry and references
At the page level, PDF.js also exposes:
- `rotate`
- `view`
- `userUnit`
- `ref`
- `pageNumber` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn358629view0Ņłéturn838487view1Ņłü

These are useful for:
- page bbox recording
- coordinate normalization
- deduplicating references
- tracing annotations/destinations back to pages

## More technical / lower-priority but still extractable

### Operator-level page content
- `getOperatorList()` gives the page operator list, which is closer to the page-description layer than ordinary text extraction ŅłĆciteŅłéturn358629view1Ņłü

### Page stats
- `stats` exists on `PDFPageProxy` when enabled ŅłĆciteŅłéturn358629view0Ņłü

### Page extraction
- `extractPages(pageInfos)` can return a saved document payload for selected pages ŅłĆciteŅłéturn358629view3Ņłü

### Page/reference mapping
- `getPageIndex(ref)`
- `cachedPageNumber(ref)` ŅłĆciteŅłéturn838487view0Ņłéturn358629view4Ņłü

Those are not ŌĆ£accessibility facts,ŌĆØ but they could be handy if you build a deeper provenance/debug layer in the manifest.

## What IŌĆÖd add to the manifest if we want richer evidence

IŌĆÖd strongly consider adding optional sections for:

- `document_identity`
  - `fingerprints`
  - `byte_length`

- `document_navigation`
  - `outline_present`
  - `destinations_present`
  - `page_labels_present`
  - `open_action_present`
  - `page_layout`
  - `page_mode`
  - `viewer_preferences`

- `document_interactivity`
  - `attachments_present`
  - `forms_present`
  - `xfa_present`
  - `javascript_present`
  - `calculation_order_present`

- `page_geometry`
  - `page_number`
  - `ref`
  - `view`
  - `rotate`
  - `user_unit`

- `optional_content`
  - `layers_present`

## The short answer

Yes. Beyond the current schema discussion, PDF.js can also extract:

- document fingerprints
- raw bytes and file size
- named destinations
- page labels
- page layout/mode/viewer prefs
- attachments
- optional content groups/layers
- more form/XFA/JavaScript details
- page geometry/ref data
- annotation subsets by type
- operator lists ŅłĆciteŅłéturn838487view0Ņłéturn838487view1Ņłéturn358629view2Ņłéturn358629view3Ņłéturn358629view4Ņłü

The big divide is:

- **useful for the review manifest**
- **useful only for deep provenance/debugging**

If you want, IŌĆÖll turn this into a **ŌĆ£PDF.js extractables inventoryŌĆØ** with columns for **field**, **API**, **likely manifest usefulness**, and **priority**.

name: Assistant
mes:
Not quite, but weŌĆÖre close to the edge of the **meaningful public extraction surface**.

Beyond what I already listed, PDF.js also exposes a few more things at the document level:

- `fingerprints` for document identity
- `getDestination(id)` and `getDestinations()` for named destinations
- `getPageLabels()` for logical/printed page labels
- `getOpenAction()`
- `getOptionalContentConfig()` for optional content groups/layers
- `getAttachments()` for embedded attachments
- `getCalculationOrderIds()` for form calculation order
- `hasJSActions()` in addition to `getJSActions()`
- `saveDocument()` and `extractPages()` for document payload export operations. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn932601view0Ņłéturn971019view0Ņłü

At the page level, in addition to text, annotations, structure tree, XFA, and operator list, PDF.js also gives you:

- `getViewport()` for rendered page geometry/transforms
- page members like `pageNumber`, `ref`, `rotate`, `userUnit`, `view`
- `stats` when enabled. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn971019view1Ņłü

So if the question is ŌĆ£is there anything else in the public API worth extracting,ŌĆØ the answer is **yes, a little**: identity, destinations, page labels, layers, attachments, calculation-order info, viewport/geometry, and some save/extract operations. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn932601view0Ņłéturn971019view0Ņłéturn971019view1Ņłü

If the question is ŌĆ£have we now basically enumerated the important public extraction capabilities,ŌĆØ then **also yes**. Past this point, youŌĆÖre mostly leaving the territory of:
- text
- structure
- forms
- annotations
- metadata
- navigation
- layout/geometry

and moving into either lower-priority viewer mechanics or unsupported/internal parsing territory. PDF.js explicitly distinguishes its supported display-layer API from its deeper core layer, and says direct core-layer use is advanced and likely to change. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn932601view0Ņłü

So the blunt version is:

- **For supported public extraction:** weŌĆÖve got most of it now.
- **For deeper raw internals:** youŌĆÖd be moving beyond the stable public API. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn932601view0Ņłéturn971019view1Ņłü

I can turn this into a final **PDF.js public extractables checklist** with three buckets: **core for our manifest**, **nice to have**, and **probably not worth modeling**.

name: Assistant
mes:
Yes ŌĆö **within the supported public PDF.js API, this is the full meaningful extraction surface for existing PDFs** that I could verify from the official docs.

IŌĆÖm scoping ŌĆ£full complete listŌĆØ to the **public Display-layer surface** on `PDFDocumentProxy` and `PDFPageProxy` that exposes information about an existing PDF. I am **excluding** rendering-only and lifecycle methods such as `render()`, `cleanup()`, and `destroy()`, and I am also excluding the deeper **Core** layer because PDF.js explicitly says it is advanced, undocumented here, and likely to change. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view3Ņłéturn710866view0Ņłéturn710866view1Ņłü

## Document-level extractables

- `numPages` ŌĆö total page count. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `fingerprints` ŌĆö document identity fingerprints; the first is always defined, the second is defined for modified PDFs. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `isPureXfa` ŌĆö whether the document is an XFA-only form. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `allXfaHtml` ŌĆö XFA HTML-like object tree, mainly for printing XFA forms. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `annotationStorage` ŌĆö storage for annotation/form data. This is more interaction/state than semantic extraction, but it is part of the public surface. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü

## Document-level methods that return information

- `getData()` ŌĆö raw PDF bytes as a `Uint8Array`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getDownloadInfo()` ŌĆö loaded file size in bytes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getMetadata()` ŌĆö Info dictionary plus metadata section. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getMarkInfo()` ŌĆö document MarkInfo flags, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getOutline()` ŌĆö bookmark/outline tree. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getDestination(id)` ŌĆö one named destination. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getDestinations()` ŌĆö all named destinations. The docs note this can be slow for large files. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getOpenAction()` ŌĆö the document open action/destination, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getPageLabels()` ŌĆö logical/printed page labels, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getPageLayout()` ŌĆö requested page layout name. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getPageMode()` ŌĆö requested page mode name. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getViewerPreferences()` ŌĆö viewer preferences object, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getPermissions()` ŌĆö permission flags, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getAttachments()` ŌĆö embedded attachments lookup table. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getOptionalContentConfig()` ŌĆö optional content groups/layers configuration. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getFieldObjects()` ŌĆö AcroForm field data, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getCalculationOrderIds()` ŌĆö IDs of annotations with calculation actions, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getJSActions()` ŌĆö document-level JavaScript actions from the name tree and catalog actions, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `hasJSActions()` ŌĆö whether some AcroForm fields have JavaScript actions. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getAnnotationsByType(types, pageIndexesToSkip)` ŌĆö selected annotation data across the document. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü

## Document/page mapping helpers

These are not semantic extraction by themselves, but they are useful for provenance and navigation:

- `getPage(pageNumber)` ŌĆö returns a `PDFPageProxy` so you can inspect a page. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getPageIndex(ref)` ŌĆö maps a page reference to zero-based page index. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `cachedPageNumber(ref)` ŌĆö returns a cached page number for a reference, if available. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü

## Page-level properties

- `pageNumber` ŌĆö page number, starting at 1. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `ref` ŌĆö reference pointing to the page. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `rotate` ŌĆö page rotation in degrees clockwise. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `userUnit` ŌĆö size of page units in 1/72 inch terms. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `view` ŌĆö visible page box as `[x1, y1, x2, y2]`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `isPureXfa` ŌĆö whether the page belongs to an XFA-only document. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `stats` ŌĆö page stats when enabled, otherwise `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü

## Page-level methods that return information

- `getTextContent(params)` ŌĆö page text content. The docs note whitespace is normalized to standard spaces. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü
- `streamTextContent(params)` ŌĆö streaming text-content extraction, with the same whitespace note. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü
- `getStructTree()` ŌĆö page structure tree, or `null` if none exists for that page. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü
- `getAnnotations(params)` ŌĆö annotation objects for the page. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view0Ņłü
- `getJSActions()` ŌĆö page-level JavaScript actions. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view0Ņłü
- `getOperatorList(params)` ŌĆö page operator list, Óż»ÓżŠÓż©ÓźĆ the drawing/content-stream operator representation. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view0Ņłü
- `getXfa()` ŌĆö fake DOM-like object tree for page XFA content, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü
- `getViewport(params)` ŌĆö page viewport with width, height, and transforms. This is layout/geometry info rather than semantics, but it is extractable and often useful. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü

## Public methods that return document payloads rather than just metadata

These are part of the public surface, though they are more export/payload operations than information-extraction:

- `saveDocument()` ŌĆö full saved document bytes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `extractPages(pageInfos)` ŌĆö extracted-page document bytes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü

## What PDF.js does **not** natively give you in this public surface

- OCR for image-only PDFs. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view3Ņłü
- A documented one-call **whole-document** structure tree method; the documented `getStructTree()` is page-level on `PDFPageProxy`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- A stable documented low-level Core-layer object API; PDF.js says the Core layer is advanced and likely to change. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view3Ņłü

## The shortest accurate summary

So yes: **PDF.js can extract a lot**. The complete meaningful public extraction surface includes:

- document identity and package info
- metadata
- MarkInfo
- outline/bookmarks
- named destinations
- page labels/layout/mode/viewer prefs
- permissions
- attachments
- optional content/layers
- form fields and calculation order
- document/page JavaScript actions
- page text
- page structure trees
- page annotations
- page operator lists
- XFA content
- page geometry and references
- raw document bytes and extracted-page bytes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłéturn240233view1Ņłéturn240233view0Ņłü

I am **excluding** rendering-only and lifecycle methods such as `render()`, `cleanup()`, and `destroy()`, and I am also excluding the deeper **Core** layer because PDF.js explicitly says it is advanced, undocumented here, and likely to change. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view3Ņłéturn710866view0Ņłéturn710866view1Ņłü

## Document-level extractables

- `numPages` ŌĆö total page count. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `fingerprints` ŌĆö document identity fingerprints; the first is always defined, the second is defined for modified PDFs. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `isPureXfa` ŌĆö whether the document is an XFA-only form. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `allXfaHtml` ŌĆö XFA HTML-like object tree, mainly for printing XFA forms. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `annotationStorage` ŌĆö storage for annotation/form data. This is more interaction/state than semantic extraction, but it is part of the public surface. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü

## Document-level methods that return information

- `getData()` ŌĆö raw PDF bytes as a `Uint8Array`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getDownloadInfo()` ŌĆö loaded file size in bytes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getMetadata()` ŌĆö Info dictionary plus metadata section. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getMarkInfo()` ŌĆö document MarkInfo flags, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getOutline()` ŌĆö bookmark/outline tree. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getDestination(id)` ŌĆö one named destination. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getDestinations()` ŌĆö all named destinations. The docs note this can be slow for large files. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getOpenAction()` ŌĆö the document open action/destination, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getPageLabels()` ŌĆö logical/printed page labels, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getPageLayout()` ŌĆö requested page layout name. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getPageMode()` ŌĆö requested page mode name. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getViewerPreferences()` ŌĆö viewer preferences object, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getPermissions()` ŌĆö permission flags, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getAttachments()` ŌĆö embedded attachments lookup table. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getOptionalContentConfig()` ŌĆö optional content groups/layers configuration. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getFieldObjects()` ŌĆö AcroForm field data, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getCalculationOrderIds()` ŌĆö IDs of annotations with calculation actions, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `getJSActions()` ŌĆö document-level JavaScript actions from the name tree and catalog actions, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- `hasJSActions()` ŌĆö whether some AcroForm fields have JavaScript actions. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getAnnotationsByType(types, pageIndexesToSkip)` ŌĆö selected annotation data across the document. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü

## Document/page mapping helpers

These are not semantic extraction by themselves, but they are useful for provenance and navigation:

- `getPage(pageNumber)` ŌĆö returns a `PDFPageProxy` so you can inspect a page. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `getPageIndex(ref)` ŌĆö maps a page reference to zero-based page index. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `cachedPageNumber(ref)` ŌĆö returns a cached page number for a reference, if available. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü

## Page-level properties

- `pageNumber` ŌĆö page number, starting at 1. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `ref` ŌĆö reference pointing to the page. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `rotate` ŌĆö page rotation in degrees clockwise. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `userUnit` ŌĆö size of page units in 1/72 inch terms. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `view` ŌĆö visible page box as `[x1, y1, x2, y2]`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `isPureXfa` ŌĆö whether the page belongs to an XFA-only document. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü
- `stats` ŌĆö page stats when enabled, otherwise `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view1Ņłü

## Page-level methods that return information

- `getTextContent(params)` ŌĆö page text content. The docs note whitespace is normalized to standard spaces. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü
- `streamTextContent(params)` ŌĆö streaming text-content extraction, with the same whitespace note. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü
- `getStructTree()` ŌĆö page structure tree, or `null` if none exists for that page. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü
- `getAnnotations(params)` ŌĆö annotation objects for the page. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view0Ņłü
- `getJSActions()` ŌĆö page-level JavaScript actions. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view0Ņłü
- `getOperatorList(params)` ŌĆö page operator list, Óż»ÓżŠÓż©ÓźĆ the drawing/content-stream operator representation. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view0Ņłü
- `getXfa()` ŌĆö fake DOM-like object tree for page XFA content, or `null`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü
- `getViewport(params)` ŌĆö page viewport with width, height, and transforms. This is layout/geometry info rather than semantics, but it is extractable and often useful. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłü

## Public methods that return document payloads rather than just metadata

These are part of the public surface, though they are more export/payload operations than information-extraction:

- `saveDocument()` ŌĆö full saved document bytes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view1Ņłü
- `extractPages(pageInfos)` ŌĆö extracted-page document bytes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłü

## What PDF.js does **not** natively give you in this public surface

- OCR for image-only PDFs. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view3Ņłü
- A documented one-call **whole-document** structure tree method; the documented `getStructTree()` is page-level on `PDFPageProxy`. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn240233view0Ņłéturn710866view0Ņłü
- A stable documented low-level Core-layer object API; PDF.js says the Core layer is advanced and likely to change. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn633882view3Ņłü

## The shortest accurate summary

So yes: **PDF.js can extract a lot**. The complete meaningful public extraction surface includes:

- document identity and package info
- metadata
- MarkInfo
- outline/bookmarks
- named destinations
- page labels/layout/mode/viewer prefs
- permissions
- attachments
- optional content/layers
- form fields and calculation order
- document/page JavaScript actions
- page text
- page structure trees
- page annotations
- page operator lists
- XFA content
- page geometry and references
- raw document bytes and extracted-page bytes. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn710866view0Ņłéturn240233view1Ņłéturn240233view0Ņłü

If you want, the next useful step is a **PDF.js ŌåÆ manifest field map**, where I pair each extractable with a proposed JSON field and note whether it is **high priority**, **nice to have**, or **probably overkill**.

## Scope

This is the **supported public PDF.js surface** for existing PDFs, mapped into a proposed **PDF ŌåÆ DOCX evidence manifest**. IŌĆÖm excluding rendering/lifecycle methods and the unstable Core layer. PDF.jsŌĆÖs public surface here comes from `PDFDocumentProxy` and `PDFPageProxy`; the projectŌĆÖs ŌĆ£Getting StartedŌĆØ page also explicitly separates the display layer from the deeper core layer. ## High priority for v0.1

These are the fields I would include first because they directly help extraction, normalization, review flags, or DOCX projection. All of these are directly documented in the public API. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn808663view0Ņłéturn682128view0Ņłéturn682128view1Ņłéturn682128view3Ņłü

| PDF.js member / method | What it gives you | Proposed manifest field | Priority | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `numPages` | Total page count | `source_package.page_count` | High | Basic document scaffold |
| `fingerprints` | Stable-ish document identity | `source_package.document_fingerprints` | High | Deduping, traceability |
| `getMetadata()` | Info dict + metadata section | `document_metadata.info`, `document_metadata.xmp_present` | High | Title/author/producer/lang clues |
| `getMarkInfo()` | MarkInfo flags | `document_accessibility.mark_info` | High | Tagged-PDF signal |
| `getOutline()` | Bookmark tree | `document_navigation.outline_entries` | High | Useful structure hints |
| `getPermissions()` | Permission flags | `document_security.permissions` | High | Access constraints, audit context |
| `getFieldObjects()` | AcroForm field data | `document_interactivity.form_fields` | High | Forms inventory |
| `getJSActions()` | Document JS actions | `document_interactivity.javascript_actions` | High | Interactive behavior flag |
| `hasJSActions()` | Whether JS actions exist | `document_interactivity.javascript_present` | High | Cheap top-level flag |
| `getPage(pageNumber)` | Page handle | `page_entries[]` driver | High | Required to inspect each page |
| `pageNumber` | 1-based page number | `page_entries[].page_number` | High | Page identity |
| `ref` | Page reference | `page_entries[].observed_source.page_ref` | High | Provenance/debugging |
| `rotate` | Page rotation | `page_entries[].observed_source.page_rotation` | High | Layout normalization |
| `userUnit` | Unit scale | `page_entries[].observed_source.user_unit` | High | Coordinate normalization |
| `view` | Page visible box | `page_entries[].observed_source.page_bbox` | High | Geometry |
| `getTextContent()` | Extracted text content | `raw_block_entries[].text`, `raw_block_entries[].text_items` | High | Core text extraction |
| `streamTextContent()` | Streaming text extraction | same as above | High | Better for large docs |
| `getStructTree()` | Page structure tree or null | `page_entries[].observed_source.struct_tree_present`, `raw_structure_entries[]` | High | Best public semantic signal |
| `getAnnotations()` | Page annotation objects | `page_entries[].annotation_entries[]` | High | Links, widgets, comments |
| `getOperatorList()` | Page operator list | `page_entries[].operator_evidence` | High | Page-description fallback |
| `getXfa()` | XFA page tree or null | `page_entries[].xfa_entries[]` | High | XFA forms/documents |
| `getViewport()` | Viewport geometry/transforms | `page_entries[].observed_source.viewport` | High | Coordinate conversion |

## Nice to have for v0.2+

These are useful and public, but I would not block the first manifest on them. They help navigation, provenance, or richer review workflows. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn808663view0Ņłéturn682128view0Ņłéturn682128view1Ņłü

| PDF.js member / method | What it gives you | Proposed manifest field | Priority | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `getData()` | Raw PDF bytes | `source_package.raw_pdf_bytes_ref` or external artifact ref | Medium | Archival/debug |
| `getDownloadInfo()` | Byte length | `source_package.byte_length` | Medium | Audit/debug |

| `getDestinations()` | All named destinations | `document_navigation.named_destinations` | Medium | Full nav graph |
| `getOpenAction()` | Open destination/action | `document_navigation.open_action` | Medium | Viewer behavior |
| `getPageLabels()` | Logical page labels | `document_navigation.page_labels` | Medium | Printed numbering vs physical pages |
| `getPageLayout()` | Requested page layout | `document_navigation.page_layout` | Medium | Viewer context |
| `getPageMode()` | Requested page mode | `document_navigation.page_mode` | Medium | Viewer context |
| `getViewerPreferences()` | Viewer preferences | `document_navigation.viewer_preferences` | Medium | Viewer context |
| `getAttachments()` | Embedded attachments | `document_embeds.attachments` | Medium | Audit/inventory |
| `getOptionalContentConfig()` | Optional content groups/layers | `document_layers.optional_content_groups` | Medium | Layered PDFs |
| `getCalculationOrderIds()` | Calc-action annotation IDs | `document_interactivity.calculation_order_ids` | Medium | Form logic |
| `getAnnotationsByType()` | Selected annotation types across doc | `document_interactivity.annotations_by_type` | Medium | Cross-doc inventory |
| `getPageIndex(ref)` | Map ref ŌåÆ page index | `provenance.page_ref_index_map` | Medium | Ref resolution |
| `cachedPageNumber(ref)` | Cached ref ŌåÆ page number | `provenance.cached_page_number_map` | Low | Debug/perf helper |
| `isPureXfa` | XFA-only doc/page flag | `document_interactivity.is_pure_xfa` / `page_entries[].is_pure_xfa` | Medium | XFA workflow split |
| `allXfaHtml` | Whole XFA HTML-ish object | `document_interactivity.all_xfa_html` | Medium | XFA export/debug |

## Probably overkill for the manifest

These are public, but I would usually keep them out of the main manifest unless you want a very deep provenance/debug artifact. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn808663view0Ņłéturn682128view1Ņłü

| PDF.js member / method | What it gives you | Proposed manifest field | Priority | Why IŌĆÖd skip it at first |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `annotationStorage` | Runtime annotation/form storage | `debug.annotation_storage` | Low | More app state than source evidence |
| `saveDocument()` | Saved full document bytes | external artifact only | Low | Output payload, not extraction fact |
| `extractPages(pageInfos)` | New bytes for selected pages | external artifact only | Low | Export utility, not manifest fact |

## What is **not** in the public extraction surface

These are the important absences.

| Need | Status in PDF.js public API | What to do |
|---|---|---|
| OCR for image-only PDFs | Not provided | Use another OCR tool |
| One-call whole-document structure tree | Not documented publicly | Assemble from page-level `getStructTree()` |
| Stable low-level object graph / COS access | Not part of supported display-layer API | Avoid for v0.1 |

PDF.jsŌĆÖs own docs say the display layer is the public surface, while the core layer is deeper and not the stable documented path here. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn682128view4Ņłéturn808663view0Ņłéturn682128view3Ņłü

## My recommended v0.1 manifest sections

This is the version IŌĆÖd actually build first:

```json id="pdf-to-docx-manifest-v0_1-fields"
{

  },

    {
      "page_number": 1,
      "observed_source": {

        "page_rotation": 0,
        "user_unit": 1,
        "page_bbox": [0, 0, 0, 0],
        "viewport": null,
        "struct_tree_present": false,
        "text_layer_detected": false,

        "xfa_present": false
      }
    }
  ],
  "raw_block_entries": [],
  "raw_structure_entries": [],
  "warning_entries": [],

}
```

## Bottom line

Yes, PDF.js can extract **enough** for a serious first-pass PDF ŌåÆ DOCX evidence manifest.

The **must-have core** is:

- page count
- fingerprints
- metadata
- MarkInfo
- outline
- permissions
- form fields
- JS presence/actions
- page geometry/ref
- text content
- page structure tree
- annotations
- operator list
- XFA content. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn808663view0Ņłéturn682128view0Ņłéturn682128view1Ņłéturn682128view3Ņłü

| OCR for image-only PDFs | Not provided | Use another OCR tool |
| One-call whole-document structure tree | Not documented publicly | Assemble from page-level `getStructTree()` |
| Stable low-level object graph / COS access | Not part of supported display-layer API | Avoid for v0.1 |

PDF.jsŌĆÖs own docs say the display layer is the public surface, while the core layer is deeper and not the stable documented path here. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn682128view4Ņłéturn808663view0Ņłéturn682128view3Ņłü

## My recommended v0.1 manifest sections

This is the version IŌĆÖd actually build first:

```json id="pdf-to-docx-manifest-v0_1-sections"
{
  "source_package": {},
  "document_metadata": {},
  "document_accessibility": {},
  "document_navigation": {},
  "document_security": {},
  "document_interactivity": {},
  "page_entries": [],
  "raw_structure_entries": [],
  "raw_block_entries": [],
  "warning_entries": [],
  "projected_target": {}
}
```

### Suggested field set for v0.1

```json id="pdf-to-docx-manifest-v0_1-fields"
{
  "source_package": {
    "input_file_name": "",
    "input_file_path": "",
    "page_count": 0,
    "document_fingerprints": [],
    "byte_length": 0
  },
  "document_metadata": {
    "info": {},
    "xmp_present": false
  },
  "document_accessibility": {
    "mark_info": null,
    "tagged_pdf_detected": false
  },
  "document_navigation": {
    "outline_entries": [],
    "page_labels": null
  },
  "document_security": {
    "permissions": null
  },
  "document_interactivity": {
    "javascript_present": false,
    "javascript_actions": null,
    "form_fields": null,
    "is_pure_xfa": false
  },
  "page_entries": [
    {
      "page_number": 1,
      "observed_source": {
        "page_ref": null,
        "page_rotation": 0,
        "user_unit": 1,
        "page_bbox": [0, 0, 0, 0],
        "viewport": null,
        "struct_tree_present": false,
        "text_layer_detected": false,
        "annotation_count": 0,
        "xfa_present": false
      }
    }
  ],
  "raw_block_entries": [],
  "raw_structure_entries": [],
  "warning_entries": [],
  "projected_target": {}
}
```

## Bottom line

Yes, PDF.js can extract **enough** for a serious first-pass PDF ŌåÆ DOCX evidence manifest.

The **must-have core** is:

- page count
- fingerprints
- metadata
- MarkInfo
- outline
- permissions
- form fields
- JS presence/actions
- page geometry/ref
- text content
- page structure tree
- annotations
- operator list
- XFA content. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn808663view0Ņłéturn682128view0Ņłéturn682128view1Ņłéturn682128view3Ņłü

If you want the next step, IŌĆÖll turn this into a literal **JSON Schema draft** for `pdf_to_docx_accessibility_manifest` v0.1.
